components.name: Grid layout
components.summary: GridLayout enables complex arrangement of UI elements within a system of columns and rows to create an evenly distributed, responsive and organized user interface. Use it to position repeating widgets in the main content area, such as in a Dashboard. When combined with responsive breakpoints, you can configure GridLayout and GridItem to determine how content is displayed across different viewports and screen sizes.
when_to_use: When it's best to display your content using a configurable number of columns and rows, such as a form or a dashboard.
For content regions where you display blocks or groups of content alongside each other, such as the main content area of the application, Dialog, or Drawer.
When you need to arrange content inside a page or app shell. Use GridLayout for the content region itself, rather than the outer shell structure.

When users will access the application or webpage across different viewport, screen or device sizes. GridLayout controls how to display the content and responsively organize across the different spaces, ensuring a reliable, usable experience.
when_not_to_use: To define the high-level layout of your application surrounding the content area (i.e., header, footer, navigation areas. Instead, use BorderLayout.

To lay out your content in a single dimension only, for example a horizontal summary bar containing a row of metric components, or a vertical stack of Accordion components. Instead, use StackLayout, FlowLayout or FlexLayout.

props.name: as
props.description: The HTML element used for the root node.

props.name: columnGap
props.description: Defines the size of the gutter between the columns by setting a density multiplier. Defaults to 1

props.name: columns
props.description: Number of columns to be displayed. Defaults to 12

props.name: gap
props.description: Defines the size of the gutter between the columns and the rows by setting a density multiplier. Defaults to 3

props.name: margin
props.description: Defines the margin around the component. It can be specified as a number (which acts as a multiplier) or a string representing the margin value. Default is 0.

props.name: padding
props.description: Defines the padding within the component. It can be specified as a number (which acts as a multiplier) or a string representing the padding value. Default is 0.

props.name: ref
props.description: No description provided.

props.name: rowGap
props.description: Defines the size of the gutter between the rows by setting a density multiplier. Defaults to 1

props.name: rows
props.description: Number of rows to be displayed. Defaults to 1

summary: When building a page layout, apply semantic HTML. This means using the correct HTML elements for their intended purpose as much as possible.
The GridLayout and GridItem components support this behavior by allowing access to a prop called as that allows you to render the appropriate HTML instead of a plain div.

rules.rule: When building a page layout, apply semantic HTML. This means using the correct HTML elements for their intended purpose as much as possible.
The GridLayout and GridItem components support this behavior by allowing access to a prop called as that allows you to render the appropriate HTML instead of a plain div.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: We recommend GridItem components for displaying content in the layout. If the layout is very simple (i.e., doesn't span multiple columns or rows), you can place content (e.g., components, text, divs, etc) directly into the GridLayout component. - Any items displayed beyond 12 columns (or the number of defined columns) will wrap to a subsequent row. - Use columnGap and rowGap properties to set a specific gap in a particular dimension or gap to update both values simultaneously.

examples.title: Columns and rows
examples.description: Use grid layout column and row properties to control the number of columns and rows in the layout.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: Use the grid layout’s responsive properties when the flow of the grid is dependent on the viewport size, such as when a user typically resizes their application or webpage and content needs to remain visible. - Define specific layout behaviors per breakpoint to optimize the content display across different device sizes.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: Spanning multiple columns and rows is an effective way to drive visual hierarchy in the layout. For example, grid items at the top of an analytical dashboard are typically larger in size to emphasize data visualizations, such as charts or metrics.

examples.title: Expanding and collapsing items
examples.description: Use the colSpan and rowSpan props to dynamically control the size of an item in the grid to show more or less content to the user. This example shows how to expand a grid item in the layout and reposition surrounding grid items at the same time.

examples.title: Positioning items
examples.description: Use the verticalAlignment and horizontalAlignment props to control the position of content in the grid layout.

examples.title: Custom columns and rows
examples.description: The columns and rows props allow you to pass a string to define a column or row template.

examples.title: Spacing
examples.description: Grid layout displays items with a gap between items that is a multiple of the Salt base unit controlled by the gap, columnGap and rowGap properties and is by default set to 3. The spacing properties padding and margin are set to 0 by default and can be used to control the layout's inner and outer spacing (also as multiples of the Salt base unit).

preferred_for: When it's best to display your content using a configurable number of columns and rows, such as a form or a dashboard.
For content regions where you display blocks or groups of content alongside each other, such as the main content area of the application, Dialog, or Drawer.
When you need to arrange content inside a page or app shell. Use GridLayout for the content region itself, rather than the outer shell structure.

When users will access the application or webpage across different viewport, screen or device sizes. GridLayout controls how to display the content and responsively organize across the different spaces, ensuring a reliable, usable experience.
not_for: To define the high-level layout of your application surrounding the content area (i.e., header, footer, navigation areas. Instead, use BorderLayout.

To lay out your content in a single dimension only, for example a horizontal summary bar containing a row of metric components, or a vertical stack of Accordion components. Instead, use StackLayout, FlowLayout or FlexLayout.

components.name: Icon
components.summary: Icon is a visual representation of an application, a capability, a concept or a specific entity with meaning on an interface (e.g., a floppy disk to represent a save action). Icons help users to recognize and understand actions that transcend linguistic or cultural boundaries. The list of available icons is in the icon foundation.
when_to_use: To aid recognition and help users make choices by providing context (e.g., an office phone number vs cell phone number in contact information).

props.name: color
props.description: No description provided.

props.name: size
props.description: Multiplier for the base icon size. Should be a positive integer to conform to the rest of the design system.

summary: Exclude decorative icons (icons that repeat the information already expressed by the accompanied text) from screen reader access to avoid repetition. If you're adding an icon for decorative purposes, pass aria-hidden=”true” to exclude its label from the screen reader.

rules.rule: Exclude decorative icons (icons that repeat the information already expressed by the accompanied text) from screen reader access to avoid repetition. If you're adding an icon for decorative purposes, pass aria-hidden=”true” to exclude its label from the screen reader.

examples.title: Basic icon
examples.description: You should individually import and use from the @salt-ds/icons package.

examples.title: Icon size
examples.description: You can change the size of each icon using the size property, which acts as a multiplier for the base icon’s size, as described in the icon foundation. The base size of the icon depends on the active density as described in the size foundation.

examples.title: Icon types
examples.description: We provide two icon types: default and solid. Most icons will have both versions, with some exceptions as described in the icon foundation. The outline style is the default icon type. Use the solid icon for additional emphasis or to represent a toggled "on" state.

examples.title: Icon color
examples.description: To override the default color of the icon, use the color prop. This allows you to set the color to primary, secondary or inherit.

examples.title: Custom icons
examples.description: If you need a custom SVG icon, use the Icon component. Icon has the same API as the native element. Whenever you add a custom icon, pass an aria-label to Icon.

examples.title: Custom colors
examples.description: If you need to use a color other than "primary" or "secondary", use color="inherit" or use CSS to change the icon's fill.

examples.title: CSS background image
examples.description: You can also use icon as background image in CSS from "@salt-ds/icons/saltIcons.css", then specify icon name prefixed with saltIcons-, e.g., saltIcons-AddDocument.

preferred_for: To aid recognition and help users make choices by providing context (e.g., an office phone number vs cell phone number in contact information).

components.name: Input
components.summary: Input provides an editable field for users to enter text using alphanumeric characters. It is ideal for short, freeform content and data entry.
when_to_use: To ask a user a subjective question to which user responses will vary and the length of their answer is likely to be less than a single line.
when_not_to_use: When the content is likely to exceed a single line. Instead, use MultilineInput. Input field width and height should always reflect the amount of content expected in the field.

props.name: bordered
props.description: Styling variant with full border. Defaults to false

props.name: emptyReadOnlyMarker
props.description: The marker to use in an empty read only Input. Use '' to disable this feature. Defaults to '—'.

props.name: endAdornment
props.description: End adornment component

props.name: inputProps
props.description: Attributes applied to the input element.

props.name: inputRef
props.description: Optional ref for the input component

props.name: readOnly
props.description: If true, the component is read only.

props.name: startAdornment
props.description: Start adornment component

props.name: textAlign
props.description: Alignment of text within container. Defaults to "left"

props.name: validationStatus
props.description: Validation status.

props.name: variant
props.description: Styling variant. Defaults to "primary".

summary: Wrap Input in FormField to provide the field with a visible label, help text, and a status message for validation feedback. This will ensure it complies with accessibility guidelines. You can find more information in the W3C form instructions.

The component clips text that extends beyond its visible area. All states have the same behavior—default, read-only, and disabled—whether or not the input has focus. To view the text in its entirety, the user can traverse the characters using the cursor.

rules.rule: Wrap Input in FormField to provide the field with a visible label, help text, and a status message for validation feedback. This will ensure it complies with accessibility guidelines. You can find more information in the W3C form instructions.

The component clips text that extends beyond its visible area. All states have the same behavior—default, read-only, and disabled—whether or not the input has focus. To view the text in its entirety, the user can traverse the characters using the cursor.

examples.title: Basic
examples.description: Input is a single-line text field that accepts user input. It can be used in forms, search bars, or anywhere you need to capture user input.

examples.title: Variants
examples.description: The Input has three variants: primary, secondary and tertiary. Each variant adjusts the background color of the field, providing options to support the creation of a visual hierarchy. By selecting the appropriate variant, you can tailor the appearance of inputs to suit different contexts and design needs.

examples.title: Bordered
examples.description: To style Input with a full border, set bordered={true}. We recommend this styling when the field uses the same fill color as the background (i.e., a primary fill color on a primary background).

examples.title: Disabled
examples.description: You can disable the input, resulting in no action when the user interacts with it.

examples.title: Read-only
examples.description: You can set Input to a read-only state. The user can't edit the value text in this state, but they can highlight and copy it. By default, a dash ('-') serves as the empty marker. You can customize this marker by using the emptyReadOnlyMarker prop if your application needs a different symbol. - Use the read-only state when the user needs the value for their flow or current task but cannot edit it, for example, because of user permissions.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: Never use placeholder text to provide the user with contextual help since it will disappear when the user starts typing and doesn't meet minimum contrast requirements. Doing so is a WCAG failure. Instead, use helper text beneath the field to provide instructions or directions necessary to complete the field, and reserve the placeholder text to support the help message, or provide an example of the suggested content. You can find more information in the W3C form instructions.

examples.title: Spellcheck
examples.description: Turn on automatic spellchecking using inputProps.

examples.title: Static adornments
examples.description: You can add custom adornments at the beginning or end of the Input field using the startAdornment and endAdornment props. Start adornments typically describe the purpose of the field (e.g., a phone number input, or a currency), while end adornments typically indicate a reveal of more information (e.g., using an arrow icon) or to trigger a new user interface (such as a date picker). You can use text and icons to add Salt styled alphanumeric text or symbols as adornments. Wrap multiple start or en

examples.title: Button adornments
examples.description: You can use Button at the beginning or end of the input field with the startAdornment and endAdornment props. Buttons can allow for custom interactivity within the input field itself or elsewhere in the app via the input field. Disable your interactive buttons or make them read-only should they match the state of the containing input field.
examples.code:
import { Button, FlowLayout, Input } from "@salt-ds/core";
import {
  CloseIcon,
  FlagIcon,
  NoteIcon,
  RefreshIcon,
  SendIcon,
} from "@salt-ds/icons";
import type { ReactElement } from "react";

export const ButtonAdornments = (): ReactElement => (
  <FlowLayout style={{ width: "266px" }}>
    <Input
      startAdornment={
        <Button>
          <NoteIcon />
        </Button>
      }
      defaultValue="Value"
    />
    <Input
      endAdornment={
        <Button sentiment="accented">
          <RefreshIcon />
        </Button>
      }
      defaultValue="Value"
    />
    <Input
      startAdornment={
        <>
          <Button>
            <SendIcon />
          </Button>
          <Button sentiment="accented">
            <FlagIcon />
          </Button>
        </>
      }
      defaultValue="Value"
    />
    <Input
      endAdornment={
        <>
          <Button appearance="transparent">
            <CloseIcon />
          </Button>
          <Button sentiment="accented">
            <FlagIcon />
          </Button>
        </>
      }
      defaultValue="Value"
    />
    <Input
      disabled
      endAdornment={
        <>
          <Button disabled>
            <SendIcon />
          </Button>
          <Button disabled appearance="transparent">
            <CloseIcon />
          </Button>
          <Button disabled sentiment="accented">
            <FlagIcon />
          </Button>
        </>
      }
      defaultValue="Value"
    />
    <Input
      readOnly
      startAdornment={
        <>
          <Button disabled>
            <SendIcon />
          </Button>
          <Button disabled appearance="transparent">
            <CloseIcon />
          </Button>
          <Button disabled sentiment="accented">
            <FlagIcon />
          </Button>
        </>
      }
      defaultValue="Value"
    />
    <Input
      disabled
      startAdornment={
        <>
          <Button disabled>
            <CloseIcon />
          </Button>
          <Button disabled appearance="transparent">
            <FlagIcon />
          </Button>
        </>
      }
      endAdornment={
        <Button sentiment="accented" disabled>
          <SendIcon />
        </Button>
      }
      defaultValue="Value"
    />
  </FlowLayout>
);

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: Use the error state to alert the user of a critical issue related to the input. This issue, which may jeopardize completion of the task, usually requires action from the user to resolve the error. - Display the warning state when you need to alert the user of a potential issue that won’t prevent them from continuing with a task but may cause errors if they don't address it.

examples.title: Text alignment
examples.description: Text has left alignment by default. Use the textAlign prop to change the alignment to “center” or “right.” Numeric values often have right alignment to make comparing them easier. However, sometimes number fields don't relate to each other or mix with text fields in a form, so left alignment may promote better visual flow. Consider your use case when deciding on input field alignment. You may choose to center-align an input field to bring emphasis to a value. However, this should be the exceptio

preferred_for: To ask a user a subjective question to which user responses will vary and the length of their answer is likely to be less than a single line.
not_for: When the content is likely to exceed a single line. Instead, use MultilineInput. Input field width and height should always reflect the amount of content expected in the field.

components.name: Kbd
components.summary: Kbd is a decorative element used to represent specific keyboard keys. It is typically embedded inside components (e.g., menu, table, button) or paragraph text, to communicate an associated keyboard shortcut or a user instruction.
when_to_use: To display keyboard shortcuts: Use Kbd to visually highlight keyboard keys or combinations (e.g., "Ctrl + S") in documentation.
Inline with instructions: When providing step-by-step instructions or help text that references keyboard actions.
In inputs: To show associated shortcuts for actions, such as “Save (Ctrl + S)” in a button or “Press Enter” in an input field.
In a menu: To associate with actions, such as the macOS Menu Bar or MS Taskbar.
To improve accessibility: When you want users to easily identify and understand available keyboard shortcuts.
For power users: Especially beneficial for users who are familiar with the application and frequently perform tasks at speed.
when_not_to_use: For non-keyboard actions: Don't use Kbd to represent mouse actions, gestures, or other non-keyboard interactions.
For non-keyboard or decorative use: Don't use Kbd for purely decorative or stylistic purposes that are unrelated to actual keyboard input.
For long text or sentences: Kbd is not suitable for wrapping entire sentences or paragraphs — use it only for individual keys or short key combinations.

props.name: variant
props.description: The variant of the kbd. Defaults to "primary".

summary: Always use Kbd alongside descriptive text that explains the associated action. For example: "Press Ctrl+K to open search" This ensures users understand the purpose of the keyboard shortcut.
For multi-key shortcuts, include clear text separators (such as “+”) between keys (e.g, "Ctrl + Shift + K"). Avoid combining keys without separators (e.g., "CtrlShiftK")

rules.rule: Always use Kbd alongside descriptive text that explains the associated action. For example: "Press Ctrl+K to open search" This ensures users understand the purpose of the keyboard shortcut.
For multi-key shortcuts, include clear text separators (such as “+”) between keys (e.g, "Ctrl + Shift + K"). Avoid combining keys without separators (e.g., "CtrlShiftK")

examples.title: Variants
examples.description: Kbd supports multiple background color variants: primary, secondary, and tertiary. The primary variant is set by default. These variants help match different UI backgrounds and provide varying levels of visual emphasis, depending on how important the shortcuts are in a given scenario.

examples.title: Inline with text
examples.description: Kbd can be used inline within text to indicate keyboard shortcuts as part of a sentence.

examples.title: Nested in the input
examples.description: Kbd can be placed inside an input field as an adornment to indicate a shortcut or expected key input

examples.title: Nested in the menu
examples.description: Kbd can be used within a menu or context menu to visually display keyboard shortcuts alongside menu items, helping users quickly identify available shortcut keys for each action.

preferred_for: To display keyboard shortcuts: Use Kbd to visually highlight keyboard keys or combinations (e.g., "Ctrl + S") in documentation.
Inline with instructions: When providing step-by-step instructions or help text that references keyboard actions.
In inputs: To show associated shortcuts for actions, such as “Save (Ctrl + S)” in a button or “Press Enter” in an input field.
In a menu: To associate with actions, such as the macOS Menu Bar or MS Taskbar.
To improve accessibility: When you want users to easily identify and understand available keyboard shortcuts.
For power users: Especially beneficial for users who are familiar with the application and frequently perform tasks at speed.
not_for: For non-keyboard actions: Don't use Kbd to represent mouse actions, gestures, or other non-keyboard interactions.
For non-keyboard or decorative use: Don't use Kbd for purely decorative or stylistic purposes that are unrelated to actual keyboard input.
For long text or sentences: Kbd is not suitable for wrapping entire sentences or paragraphs — use it only for individual keys or short key combinations.

components.name: Link
components.summary: Link allows users to navigate to a different site or to other pages within the current site. You can use link on its own, or inline within or after a sentence.
when_to_use: To provide navigation to a page on the same or different site.
To link to documents, email addresses or phone numbers.
To send users to a specific section on the same page.
To provide users with more detailed information or assistance e.g., terms and conditions, help sections or contact information.
when_not_to_use: To trigger an action, such as submitting a form or opening a dialog. Instead, use Button.

props.name: color
props.description: No description provided.

props.name: IconComponent
props.description: Icon component displayed for external links. Defaults to ExternalIcon from SemanticIconProvider.

props.name: maxRows
props.description: Apply text truncation by mentioning number of rows to be displayed

props.name: render
props.type: ReactElement<any, string | JSXElementConstructor<any>> | ((props: any) => ReactElement<any, string | JSXElementConstructor<any>>)
props.description: Render prop to enable customisation of anchor element.

props.name: styleAs
props.description: Match styling to another text component's style

props.name: underline
props.description: Either "default" or "never". Determines when underline should be applied to the link.

props.name: variant
props.description: Change text color palette

summary: Screen reader users will use the Tab key to jump from link to link or pull up a list of links on a page. Avoid using the same link text to link to different content and ensure that links make sense out of context.
When a link contains an icon and text, pass aria-hidden=”true" to the icon so that its label is not announced by screen readers.
When a link has an icon and no text, pass an aria-label to the link to describe the content that the link leads to.

rules.rule: Screen reader users will use the Tab key to jump from link to link or pull up a list of links on a page. Avoid using the same link text to link to different content and ensure that links make sense out of context.
When a link contains an icon and text, pass aria-hidden=”true" to the icon so that its label is not announced by screen readers.
When a link has an icon and no text, pass an aria-label to the link to describe the content that the link leads to.

examples.title: Default
examples.description: By default, a link will only navigate the user within the same tab, replacing the page where the user followed the link.

examples.title: Security
examples.description: When linking to third party content, we recommended setting rel=”noopener” or rel=”noreferrer”. Although this is default behavior in newer browsers, explicitly specifying this helps protect users of legacy browsers. For more information, read “Links to cross-origin destinations are unsafe”.

examples.title: Color
examples.description: To help create a visual hierarchy using a link, use the color prop. The default color is primary. When color is set to "inherit" Link's hover, active and focus colors will also be inherited.
examples.code:
import { Link, StackLayout, Text } from "@salt-ds/core";
import type { ReactElement } from "react";
import styles from "./index.module.css";

export const Color = (): ReactElement => (
  <StackLayout align="start">
    <Link href="#" color="accent" className={styles.linkExample}>
      Accent Link color
    </Link>
    <Link href="#" color="primary" className={styles.linkExample}>
      Primary Link color
    </Link>
    <Link href="#" color="secondary" className={styles.linkExample}>
      Secondary Link color
    </Link>
    <Text color="error">
      <Link href="#" color="inherit" className={styles.linkExample}>
        Inherit Link color
      </Link>
    </Text>
  </StackLayout>
);

examples.title: Visited
examples.description: Links change color after a user clicks on them. This tells the user which links they have already visited.

examples.title: Underline
examples.description: By default, links are underlined apart from hover state. If you need a more subtle effect, set underline="never".

examples.title: Render prop - element
examples.description: Using the render prop, you can customize the element rendered by the Link. Props defined on the JSX element will be merged with props from the Link.

examples.title: Render prop - callback
examples.description: The render prop can also accept a function. This approach allows more control over how props are merged, allowing for more precise customization of the component's behavior. When a function is passed to the render prop, it's your responsibility to merge props within the function itself.

examples.title: Variant
examples.description: **Note:** This prop is deprecated. Use the color prop instead.

preferred_for: To provide navigation to a page on the same or different site.
To link to documents, email addresses or phone numbers.
To send users to a specific section on the same page.
To provide users with more detailed information or assistance e.g., terms and conditions, help sections or contact information.
not_for: To trigger an action, such as submitting a form or opening a dialog. Instead, use Button.

components.name: List box
components.summary: ListBox allows the user to select an item from an array of options, where selected items are visually distinct from non-selected items. To ensure efficient space usage, long lists of items are in a scrolling pane providing access to options not immediately visible.
when_to_use: To present a set of selectable options that allow users to make a choice for an onward action, such as filtering a data grid, selecting a country or assigning a task.
when_not_to_use: If the list items provide navigation, consider using stacked NavigationItems to form a vertical navigation pattern.
If the list is likely to contain five items or fewer, consider using RadioButtonGroup.
If the list is likely to contain between five and 10 items and doesn't need to be visible at all times, consider placing the list of items within Dropdown.
If the list contains more than 10 items and you have limited screen space, consider using a ComboBox.

props.name: bordered
props.description: If false, the list box will have not a border.

props.name: children
props.description: The options to display in the list box.

props.name: defaultOpen
props.description: If true, the list will be open by default.

props.name: defaultSelected
props.description: The default selected options. If this is provided defaultValue should be provided as well.

props.name: disabled
props.description: If true, the list box will be disabled. If true, the control will be disabled.

props.name: multiselect
props.description: If true, the list box will be multiselect. If true, multiple options can be selected.

props.name: onSelectionChange
props.description: Callback fired when the selected options change.

props.name: open
props.description: If true, the list will be open. Useful for controlling the component.

props.name: readOnly
props.description: If true, the control will be read-only.

props.name: ref
props.description: No description provided.

props.name: selected
props.description: The selected options. The component will be controlled if this prop is provided.

props.name: valueToString
props.description: Callback used to convert an option's value to a string. This is needed when the value is different to the display value or the value is not a string.

examples.title: Single-select
examples.description: The default list allows a user to make a single selection from the options available.

examples.title: Multiselect
examples.description: When multiselect={true}, users can select multiple items from the list box options.

examples.title: Grouped options
examples.description: Wrap options in a OptionGroup component to group them together. Use the label prop to provide a label for the group.

examples.title: Disabled option
examples.description: Use the disabled prop on the Option component to disable an option. Disabled options are not selectable and are visually distinct from selectable options.

examples.title: Bordered
examples.description: Use the bordered prop to add a border to the list box.

examples.title: Scrolling
examples.description: The list box will scroll when the number of options exceeds the height of the list box.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: Users can benefit from seeing a particular visual with list items. Customize the display as you see fit. For example, if displaying a list of contacts, it may help to provide avatars alongside the option text. - Place any visual support such as icons, country symbols or avatars to the left of the option text.

preferred_for: To present a set of selectable options that allow users to make a choice for an onward action, such as filtering a data grid, selecting a country or assigning a task.
not_for: If the list items provide navigation, consider using stacked NavigationItems to form a vertical navigation pattern.
If the list is likely to contain five items or fewer, consider using RadioButtonGroup.
If the list is likely to contain between five and 10 items and doesn't need to be visible at all times, consider placing the list of items within Dropdown.
If the list contains more than 10 items and you have limited screen space, consider using a ComboBox.

components.name: Localization provider
components.summary: LocalizationProvider configures the locale and date adapter for components with date objects.
when_to_use: When you use DatePicker, DateInput or Calendar components.
When you want to configure your own date adapter.
When you want to change the locale to non-US.
When you want to use a legacy data adapter, such as Moment as part of a migration journey to a maintained adapter.
when_not_to_use: Inside an application that has no date controls.

props.name: children
props.description: The child components to be rendered within the provider.

props.name: DateAdapter
props.description: The date adapter class, which provides methods for date manipulation and formatting. This should be a constructor for a class implementing the SaltDateAdapter interface.

props.name: instance
props.description: The instance of the date library being used.

props.name: locale
props.description: The locale to be used for date formatting and manipulation.

props.name: maxDate
props.description: The maximum date allowed for all date selections. Defaults to December 31, 2099.

props.name: minDate
props.description: The minimum date allowed for all date selections. Defaults to January 1, 1900.

examples.title: Adapters
examples.description: To integrate your date framework with Salt components and enable support for locales or timezones, defining a date adapter is essential. Salt provides date adapters for the most popular date frameworks, which you can extend or use as a foundation to create your own custom adapter. - Luxon: A modern successor to Moment.js, it returns Luxon objects - **Recommended for all J.P. Morgan use-cases**. - Moment.js: A legacy library that is no longer actively maintained. - Provided to assist J.P.Morgan m

examples.title: Min/Max Dates
examples.description: Use LocalizationProvider to change the min/max dates for the date adapter. These represent the fallback dates when no min/max date is provided by the component. In this example, date selection is limited to 3 months.

examples.title: Locales
examples.description: The LocalizationProvider defines the locale used by Salt's date based controls such as Calendar, DateInput and DatePicker. Configuration of locales is date framework specific and may require you to import specific locales from the date library you are using. By default, Salt defaults to the "enUS" locale (or equivalent).

preferred_for: When you use DatePicker, DateInput or Calendar components.
When you want to configure your own date adapter.
When you want to change the locale to non-US.
When you want to use a legacy data adapter, such as Moment as part of a migration journey to a maintained adapter.
not_for: Inside an application that has no date controls.

components.name: Menu
components.summary: Menu is a container that presents a selection of choices or actions from which to choose. The user opens it with a customizable trigger, such as an interactive button, or an interaction, such as a keyboard shortcut or mouse right-click.
when_to_use: When you need to display a list of actions or options.
When you have limited space and need to display overflow action in a list.
When you need to display secondary or less important information and actions inside a trigger element.
when_not_to_use: Inside a form. Instead, use ComboBox or Dropdown.

props.name: defaultOpen
props.description: If true, the menu will be open by default.

props.name: getVirtualElement
props.description: Function that returns a virtual element. If this is provided, it will override MenuTrigger.

props.name: onOpenChange
props.description: Callback function triggered when open state changes.

props.name: open
props.description: Display or hide the component.

props.name: placement
props.description: Set the placement of the Menu component relative to the trigger element. Defaults to bottom-start if it's the root menu or right-start if it's nested.

props.name: children
props.description: Menus to be rendered inside the menu group.

props.name: label
props.description: The label of the menu group.

props.name: disabled
props.description: If true, the item will be disabled.

props.name: children
props.description: The content of the menu panel.

props.name: children
props.description: The trigger element for the menu. This should be a single child element and accept a ref.

examples.title: Single level
examples.description: Clicking on the menu trigger will display a list of actions that the user can take.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: Creating submenus as separate components will allow you to reuse them in different parts of your application and make your code more maintainable. - Limit the number of levels in your menu to avoid overwhelming users.

examples.title: Grouped items
examples.description: Menu items can optionally be grouped together to organize related actions using a group label and separator.

examples.title: Separator only
examples.description: A MenuGroup can be used without a label prop to visually separate groups of menu items.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: Icons should be displayed before a menu item label. - Use icons consistently across all menu items within the same group.

examples.title: With badge
examples.description: You can add a badge to a menu item to serve as a visual cue. A badge can inform users of additional context, notifications, signpost new content or indicate a status.

examples.title: Placement
examples.description: Placement of the menu can be specified using placement prop. The menu will be positioned relative to child of the MenuTrigger component. By default, the root menu is aligned to the "bottom-start" of the trigger element and submenus to the "right-start" of the parent menu item.

examples.title: Scrolling
examples.description: When a menu extends beyond the application window, the menu defaults to span the maximum height of the viewport and scroll.

examples.title: Virtual element
examples.description: A Menu can be shown relative to a virtual element using the getVirtualElement prop. This can be used to create a context menu.

examples.title: Descriptions
examples.description: You can add descriptions to provide extra context using the Text and Stack Layout components.

preferred_for: When you need to display a list of actions or options.
When you have limited space and need to display overflow action in a list.
When you need to display secondary or less important information and actions inside a trigger element.
not_for: Inside a form. Instead, use ComboBox or Dropdown.

components.name: Multiline input
components.summary: MultilineInput provides an editable text area where users can enter multiple lines of alphanumeric characters, while retaining visibility of what they've written.
when_to_use: Use a multiline input when asking a user a subjective question and the length of their answer is unknown. The input can give them plenty of space to answer the question and review the answer. If you can take an educated guess at the possible length of their answer, set the height accordingly—see the "Number of rows" example.
when_not_to_use: Multiline input field width and height should always reflect the amount of content expected in the field. If the content is unlikely to exceed a single line, always use Input.

props.name: bordered
props.description: Styling variant with full border. Defaults to false

props.name: endAdornment
props.description: End adornment component

props.name: readOnly
props.description: If true, the component is read only.

props.name: rows
props.description: The default minimum number of rows. Defaults to 3

props.name: startAdornment
props.description: Start adornment component

props.name: textAreaProps
props.description: Attributes applied to the textarea element.

props.name: textAreaRef
props.description: Optional ref for the textarea component

props.name: validationStatus
props.description: Validation status.

props.name: variant
props.description: Styling variant. Defaults to "primary".

summary: Wrap MultilineInput in FormField to provide the field with a visible label, help text and a status message for validation feedback. This will ensure it complies with accessibility guidelines. You can find more information in the W3C form instructions.

A multiline input will clip text that extends beyond its visible area. This behavior is common to all states, i.e., default, read-only and disabled—whether or not the input has focus. To view the text in its entirety, the user can traverse the characters using the cursor.

rules.rule: Wrap MultilineInput in FormField to provide the field with a visible label, help text and a status message for validation feedback. This will ensure it complies with accessibility guidelines. You can find more information in the W3C form instructions.

A multiline input will clip text that extends beyond its visible area. This behavior is common to all states, i.e., default, read-only and disabled—whether or not the input has focus. To view the text in its entirety, the user can traverse the characters using the cursor.

examples.title: Basic
examples.description: MultilineInput is a text input field that allows the user to enter multiple lines of text. Values entered into the multiline input wrap based on the width of the component. By default, the component presents three rows of text at a time. If entering more than three rows of text, the height of the input will grow with the content unless resized by the user, that can then review the entered value using keyboard controls.

examples.title: Variants
examples.description: The MultilineInput has three variants: primary, secondary and tertiary. This changes the background color of the field and can be used to create a visual hierarchy.

examples.title: Bordered
examples.description: To style MultilineInput with a full border, set bordered={true}. We recommend this styling when the field uses the same fill color as the background (i.e., a primary fill color on a primary background).

examples.title: Disabled
examples.description: You can set MultilineInput to a disabled state, with no resultant action when the user interacts with it. Showing it in a disabled state means the user is aware of its presence or an entered value but is unable to edit it.

examples.title: Read-only
examples.description: You can set MultilineInput to a read-only state. The user cannot edit entered text in this state, but they can highlight and copy it. Use the read-only state when the value is necessary for the user’s flow or current task, but you want to restrict editing, for instance, when a user has restricted permissions. When setting MultilineInput to a read-only state, ensure to also disable any nested interactive adornments, such as buttons.

examples.title: Number of rows
examples.description: The multiline input shows three rows of text by default. You can adjust this using the rows prop to provide a larger input, allowing the user to review more of the entered text without the need for further interactions. This example demonstrates a multiline input customized to show four rows. Set the number of rows to best reflect the context of your multiline input and how much user input you expect in that context.

examples.title: Character count
examples.description: You can add a character count as a label using endAdornment props. The example demonstrates the expected behavior when the user exceeds the character count, with the field dynamically displaying an error state, and the count continuing to display the number of characters over the limit. If the user goes over the character count limit, display an error state and change the Label to primary variant with a weight of "strong".

examples.title: Validation status
examples.description: Indicate a validation state by setting the validationStatus prop. - Use the error state when you want to alert the user to a critical issue related to the input. This issue, which will jeopardize completion of the task, usually requires action from the user to resolve the error. - Use the warning state when you need to alert the user of a noncritical issue that won’t prevent them from continuing with the task but may cause errors if they don't address it.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: Never use placeholder text to provide the user with contextual help since it will disappear when the user starts typing and doesn't meet minimum contrast requirements. Doing so is a WCAG failure. Instead, use helper text beneath the input to provide instructions and reserve the placeholder text to support the help message or provide an example of the input. You can find more information in this W3 tutorial.

examples.title: Static adornments
examples.description: You can add custom adornments at the beginning or end of the multiline input area using the startAdornment and endAdornment props. Start adornments typically describe the purpose of the field (e.g., a phone number input, or a currency), whereas end adornments typically indicate a reveal of more information (e.g., using an arrow icon) or trigger a new user interface, such as a date picker. You can use Salt text and icons to add Salt styled alphanumeric text or symbols as adornments. You should wr

examples.title: Button adornments
examples.description: You can use a button at the beginning or end of the multiline input using the startAdornment and endAdornment props. Buttons can allow for custom interactivity within the multiline input area itself, or elsewhere in the app via the multiline input. If you've disabled the containing multiline input or set it to a read-only state, ensure your interactive buttons match that state.

examples.title: Max rows
examples.description: To control the number of maximum rows visible, you can set a max height for the multiline input.

preferred_for: Use a multiline input when asking a user a subjective question and the length of their answer is unknown. The input can give them plenty of space to answer the question and review the answer. If you can take an educated guess at the possible length of their answer, set the height accordingly—see the "Number of rows" example.
not_for: Multiline input field width and height should always reflect the amount of content expected in the field. If the content is unlikely to exceed a single line, always use Input.

components.name: Navigation item
components.summary: NavigationItem allows users to navigate between discrete applications and/or web pages.
when_to_use: When you have multiple levels of navigation in your application or website.

We suggest first considering a horizontal group of navigation items for distinct webpages or applications. If you have multiple (more than eight) services to navigate to at a platform level, or have navigation across distinct apps, consider using a vertical group instead.
If you need your items to remain visible on scroll, we recommend displaying navigation items in a fixed app header (or header area).
when_not_to_use: When you are creating new vertical navigation. Use VerticalNavigation instead.
When you need to show different views of content within the same page. Instead, use Tabs.

When your content is very specific. In this case, a minimalistic or single-page design may be more appropriate than a complex navigation structure. Instead, use InteractableCard, or Link within the body of the page.

props.name: active
props.description: Whether the navigation item is active.

props.name: blurActive
props.description: Whether the nested group is collapsed and there is an active nested item within it.

props.name: expanded
props.description: Whether the navigation item is expanded.

props.name: href
props.description: Href to be passed to the Link element.

props.name: level
props.description: Level of nesting.

props.name: onExpand
props.description: Action to be triggered when the navigation item is expanded.

props.name: orientation
props.description: The orientation of the navigation item.

props.name: parent
props.description: Whether the navigation item is a parent with nested items.

props.name: render
props.type: ReactElement<any, string | JSXElementConstructor<any>> | ((props: any) => ReactElement<any, string | JSXElementConstructor<any>>)
props.description: Render prop to enable customisation of navigation item element.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: Use a horizontal group of navigation items when you have fewer than eight items, otherwise consider a vertical group of items.

examples.title: Vertical group
examples.description: A vertical navigation group provides a simple, single-level list of links that users can select within an application or website.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: If a navigation item has nested items within it, it will act as a trigger to show and hide the nested items. A parent item cannot act as a link and navigate to an overview page. If you require an overview page, use the first nested item to achieve this.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: Ensure that you use icon descriptors consistently across all items within the same hierarchy level. This helps keep them equally weighted.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: Only place a badge in a navigation item that does not include nested items, i.e., it will not have a chevron.

preferred_for: When you have multiple levels of navigation in your application or website.

We suggest first considering a horizontal group of navigation items for distinct webpages or applications. If you have multiple (more than eight) services to navigate to at a platform level, or have navigation across distinct apps, consider using a vertical group instead.
If you need your items to remain visible on scroll, we recommend displaying navigation items in a fixed app header (or header area).
not_for: When you are creating new vertical navigation. Use VerticalNavigation instead.
When you need to show different views of content within the same page. Instead, use Tabs.

When your content is very specific. In this case, a minimalistic or single-page design may be more appropriate than a complex navigation structure. Instead, use InteractableCard, or Link within the body of the page.

Use VerticalNavigation for new vertical navigation. NavigationItem can remain in existing vertical implementations during migration, but we recommend moving to VerticalNavigation because it provides a more structured approach and NavigationItem vertical usage will be deprecated in a future release.

components.name: Number input
components.summary: NumberInput displays a default numeric value that users can increase or decrease using the controls or with keyboard actions. Users can also manually enter a specific value. The component is useful for scenarios where users need to make small adjustments to a value within a well-defined range.
when_to_use: To adjust a value in small, precise increments or decrements, such as setting the quantity of items, adjusting a numerical setting, or specifying a time duration.
When the value has a clear minimum and maximum limit, and you want to prevent users from entering values outside this range.
When the input is purely numerical and does not require complex formatting.
If a user benefits from adjusting values without having to type them manually, especially on touch interfaces where typing can be cumbersome.
If there is a requirement to provide immediate visual feedback as the user adjusts the value, helping them understand the impact of their changes in real-time.
when_not_to_use: When users need the flexibility to enter any (subjective) value, including those outside a predefined range. Instead, use Input or Multiline input.
When users need to select a single date or select a date range, between a start and end date. Instead, use Date input.
When users need to select an item from a large list of options without scrolling. Instead, use Combo box.
To choose one value from a set of five to ten options that does not require filtering. Instead, use Dropdown.

props.name: bordered
props.description: Styling variant with full border.

props.name: clamp
props.description: A boolean that, when true, ensures the input value is clamped within the specified min and max range upon losing focus.

props.name: decimalScale
props.description: Number of decimal places allowed (only used by default parser/formatter). Defaults to the decimal scale of either the initial value provided or the step, whichever is greater. For high precision of larger numbers, consider a custom parser/formatter from a third-party library.

props.name: decrement
props.type: (value: string, step: number, stepMultiplier: number) => string
props.description: Custom decrement function to override the default increment behavior, use when higher precision or large numbers are required value: current value step: step value stepMultiplier: step multiplier value, when using shift key navigation

props.name: defaultValue
props.description: The default value. Use when the component is uncontrolled.

props.name: disabled
props.description: Disable the NumberInput.

props.name: emptyReadOnlyMarker
props.description: The marker to use in an empty read only Input.

props.name: endAdornment
props.description: End adornment component.

props.name: format
props.description: Callback to format the NumberInput value. For high precision or large numbers, consider creating a custom parser/format using a third-party library.

props.name: hideButtons
props.description: Hide the number buttons.

props.name: increment
props.type: (value: string, step: number, stepMultiplier: number) => string
props.description: Custom increment function to override the default increment behavior, use when higher precision or large numbers are required value: current value step: step value stepMultiplier: step multiplier value, when using shift key navigation

props.name: inputProps
props.description: Attributes applied to the input element.

props.name: inputRef
props.description: Optional ref for the input component.

props.name: max
props.description: The maximum value that can be selected.

props.name: min
props.description: The minimum value that can be selected.

props.name: onChange
props.description: Callback function that is triggered when the value changes via user input or increment/decrement. Use onNumberChange if you want stable number, after blur or through increment/decrement @param event - The event that triggers the value change, can be null if called by long-press of increment/decrement @param value - value as string

props.name: onNumberChange
props.description: Callback function that is triggered when the value changes via increment/decrement or on blur. @param event - The event that triggers the change, can be null if called by long-press of increment/decrement @param value - The committed, parsed number value or null if an empty value

props.name: parse
props.description: Callback to parse the NumberInput value, used with the format callback. For high precision or large numbers, consider creating a custom parser/format using a third-party library. * Return null for empty input.

props.name: pattern
props.description: Callback that matches on values as you type and determines whether the value can be entered.

props.name: placeholder
props.description: A string displayed in a dimmed color when the NumberInput value is empty.

props.name: readOnly
props.description: A boolean property that controls the read-only state of the NumberInput. - When set to true, the NumberInput becomes read-only, preventing user edits. - When set to false or omitted, the NumberInput is editable by the user.

props.name: startAdornment
props.description: Start adornment component.

props.name: step
props.description: The amount to increment or decrement the value by when using the NumberInput buttons or Up Arrow and Down Arrow keys.

props.name: stepMultiplier
props.description: Defines the factor by which the step value is multiplied to determine the maximum increment or decrement when the Shift key is held while pressing the Up Arrow or Down Arrow keys for faster adjustments of the value.

props.name: textAlign
props.description: Specifies the alignment of the text within the NumberInput.

props.name: validationStatus
props.description: Validation status.

props.name: value
props.description: Value of the NumberInput, to be used when in a controlled state.

props.name: variant
props.description: Styling variant.

summary: Wrap NumberInput in FormField to provide the field with a visible label, help text and a status message for validation feedback. This will ensure it complies with accessibility guidelines. You can find more information in the W3C form instructions.

rules.rule: Wrap NumberInput in FormField to provide the field with a visible label, help text and a status message for validation feedback. This will ensure it complies with accessibility guidelines. You can find more information in the W3C form instructions.

examples.title: Default
examples.description: The NumberInput has a default increment step of 1, which is adjustable via the step prop for precise value changes. For larger adjustments, users can hold the Shift key while using the arrow keys to apply a stepMultiplier, a prop that defaults to 2. The parsed numeric value can be accessed through the onNumberChange event handler.

examples.title: Variants
examples.description: The NumberInput has three variants: primary, secondary and tertiary. Each variant adjusts the background color of the input. the default variant is primary but can be switched using the variant prop. When deciding between the variants, take into account the background color where the component will be displayed.

examples.title: Bordered
examples.description: By default, the NumberInput is transparent. You can add borders using the bordered prop.

examples.title: Disabled
examples.description: The NumberInput can be disabled to prevent any user interaction or input.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: Use the read-only state when users need to view the value for their workflow or current task but are not permitted to edit it, such as due to user permissions.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: Right alignment is often used for numeric values to facilitate easier comparison. However, if number fields are mixed with text fields or do not relate to each other, left alignment may enhance the overall visual flow. - Center alignment can be used to emphasize a particular value, but it should be applied sparingly and only in exceptional cases to avoid disrupting the visual consistency.
examples.code:
import {
  FormField,
  FormFieldLabel,
  NumberInput,
  StackLayout,
} from "@salt-ds/core";

export const TextAlignment = () => (
  <StackLayout style={{ width: "256px" }}>
    <FormField>
      <FormFieldLabel>Left aligned</FormFieldLabel>
      <NumberInput defaultValue={100} textAlign="left" />
    </FormField>
    <FormField>
      <FormFieldLabel>Center aligned</FormFieldLabel>
      <NumberInput defaultValue={100} textAlign="center" />
    </FormField>
    <FormField>
      <FormFieldLabel>Right aligned</FormFieldLabel>
      <NumberInput defaultValue={100} textAlign="right" />
    </FormField>
  </StackLayout>
);

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: Clearly communicate the accepted value range by utilizing the FormField's helper text to guide users. - Use the FormField error state to alert users when they manually enter a value outside the permitted range, indicating that the input is invalid. - Use the clamp prop to enforce the value to be within range.

examples.title: Clamp behavior
examples.description: By default, even when minimum and maximum values are specified, they are not strictly enforced, allowing users to type values outside this range. However, by setting the prop clamp to true, the input will automatically adjust to the nearest minimum or maximum value when the input field loses focus. **Note:** When using the clamp prop, ensure that the screen reader is updated to announce the clamped value, providing an accessible experience for users relying on assistive technologies.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: **Error state**: Use the error state to notify users of a critical issue related to the input. This issue may hinder task completion and typically requires user intervention to resolve. - **Warning state**: Use the warning state to caution users about a potential issue. While this issue won't stop users from proceeding with the task, it may lead to errors if not addressed.

examples.title: Thousands separator
examples.description: To make large numbers easier to read, you can use a combination of format and parse callbacks to insert commas and separate thousands.

examples.title: Compact notation
examples.description: You can use format to convert the numerical value into its compact notation and parse to convert compact notation back to its numerical value. This is useful when used with charts or other forms of data visualization. The example below supports the following compact notations: The example below supports the following conversion: - Thousands to K - Millions to M - Billions to B - Trillions to T

examples.title: With suffix or prefix
examples.description: You can add a suffix or prefix with a custom format function.

examples.title: Custom input pattern
examples.description: You can support custom input patterns, such as "10x", using a combination of pattern, format and parse callbacks.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: When dealing with currencies such as pounds sterling or U.S. dollars in a retail context, limit the decimal places to two, as these currencies typically require only two decimal places for accuracy. Avoid using three decimal places in such scenarios to maintain consistency and relevance.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: When using the default parser and formatter, always use the onNumberChange event to obtain the parsed numeric value and handle precision appropriately in your application logic.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: Make sure the step value is suitable for the context. For example, when users are inputting a "Number of people," increments of 0.5 may cause confusion. If unsure, maintain the step at 1 to ensure clarity and simplicity.

examples.title: Button adornments
examples.description: You can add a custom Button at the end of the NumberInput using the endAdornment prop. This allows for custom interactivity within the input field. When using endAdornment buttons for tasks such as reset or synchronization, it is essential to provide immediate feedback to users, especially those using screen readers. Implementing an aria-live region ensures that screen readers announce dynamic content changes, keeping users informed about actions such as resetting or synchronizing the input fiel

preferred_for: To adjust a value in small, precise increments or decrements, such as setting the quantity of items, adjusting a numerical setting, or specifying a time duration.
When the value has a clear minimum and maximum limit, and you want to prevent users from entering values outside this range.
When the input is purely numerical and does not require complex formatting.
If a user benefits from adjusting values without having to type them manually, especially on touch interfaces where typing can be cumbersome.
If there is a requirement to provide immediate visual feedback as the user adjusts the value, helping them understand the impact of their changes in real-time.
not_for: When users need the flexibility to enter any (subjective) value, including those outside a predefined range. Instead, use Input or Multiline input.
When users need to select a single date or select a date range, between a start and end date. Instead, use Date input.
When users need to select an item from a large list of options without scrolling. Instead, use Combo box.
To choose one value from a set of five to ten options that does not require filtering. Instead, use Dropdown.

For filter or dropdown panels that overlay content.
When acknowledgement on overlay content is needed. An overlay will only dismiss after user interaction.
when_not_to_use: To notify and present critical information that requires immediate action from the user. Instead, use Dialog.
To provide a non-focusable and informative hint. Instead, use Tooltip.

examples.title: Default overlay

examples.title: Header
examples.description: OverlayHeader follows our standardized header for container components and app regions, and it can be added as a child of the OverlayPanel component to provide a structured header for overlay. The header includes a title and actions that follows our Header Block pattern. This approach is recommended over using the OverlayPanelCloseButton separately.

examples.title: Long content

examples.title: With actions

examples.title: Deprecated Close button
examples.description: OverlayPanelCloseButton can be added as a child of the OverlayPanel component to render a close button in the top right corner of the OverlayPanel. You can pass the onClick prop like with any Button component to handle the close button click. **Note:** OverlayPanelCloseButton is deprecated. Use the prop actions within OverlayHeader instead.

components.name: Pagination
components.summary: Pagination allows users navigate easily between multiple pages of content. It enables users to jump to specific points within a set of results, helping to focus their attention and provide a sense of scale.
when_to_use: When presenting a large amount of content that may take time to load, or when users need to review and navigate to content that’s split across sequential pages.
To organize and present search results or data within a grid.

To provide a natural break point that allows users to determine if they’ve found what they need when viewing the results of a search or data query, or if they must navigate to another page or refine their initial query.
To enable users to reference specific pages within a set of results.
when_not_to_use: When you’re walking the user through a sequential process, such as an onboarding form. Instead, use Stepper to communicate current progress and the button bar pattern to provide navigation.

When there’s no need for users to navigate between, or filter, results. Instead, use infinite scrolling. This allows users to scroll through a large amount of content with no clear end point, adding more content to the page as the user scrolls. For example, a common use case is for news articles or social site feeds, where searching or filtering isn’t an expected behavior and the content has a flat hierarchy.

When you want to present content in clearly defined batches without defining the total number of pages. Instead, use lazy loading. A **Load More** button loads the next set of results on the same page—giving the user greater control.

props.name: count
props.description: Number of pages.

props.name: defaultPage
props.description: Default current/active page.

props.name: onPageChange
props.description: Callback function triggered when current page changed.

props.name: page
props.description: Current/active page.

examples.title: Default
examples.description: Use the default component to navigate a small number of pages. The user can navigate in single page increments or jump directly to a page in a set range.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: Include the input field when pagination is likely to total hundreds or thousands of pages. - Order results logically, such as in a chronological order. This helps users to navigate through several pages at a time. - Place the input to the left of the pagination range if users will use it regularly. Otherwise, position it to the right.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: Consider this variant when space is limited. - Position the compact component appropriately for your UI. Use left aligned for pages of text results, center for pages of images or equally-sized tiles, and right aligned for a full-width data grid.

examples.title: Compact with input
examples.description: Use CompactInput component to provide the ability to jump to a specific page number.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: Consider the number of likely displayed results and the available space when configuring the width of your pagination. Although truncation helps minimize the component's width, a range containing hundreds or thousands of pages will inevitably be wider than a range containing tens of pages. - Allow space for the width to change, when setting horizontal alignment, as it may impact other surrounding elements.

examples.title: Sibling pair count
examples.description: You can configure the visible page range using the siblingCount prop—increasing or decreasing the sibling range, i.e., pages on either side of the active page, depending on the breakpoint. Always make changes to sibling pages in sets of two to ensure that your page range is an odd number. This keeps the active page centred within the range and visually balanced.

examples.title: Boundary count
examples.description: You can configure the visible page range using the boundaryCount prop—increasing or decreasing the number of pages displayed next to the arrow buttons. The minimum value is 1, so there will always be at least one item next to the arrow buttons.

examples.title: Compact with GoToInput
examples.description: You can use a combination of CompactPaginator and GoToInput if you want a compressed version of pagination that also provides an input with a label.

To provide a natural break point that allows users to determine if they’ve found what they need when viewing the results of a search or data query, or if they must navigate to another page or refine their initial query.
To enable users to reference specific pages within a set of results.
not_for: When you’re walking the user through a sequential process, such as an onboarding form. Instead, use Stepper to communicate current progress and the button bar pattern to provide navigation.

When there’s no need for users to navigate between, or filter, results. Instead, use infinite scrolling. This allows users to scroll through a large amount of content with no clear end point, adding more content to the page as the user scrolls. For example, a common use case is for news articles or social site feeds, where searching or filtering isn’t an expected behavior and the content has a flat hierarchy.

components.name: Panel
components.summary: Panel is a background that organizes content in an application. It uses color to reflect level of importance and convey visual hierarchy.
when_to_use: To organize and divide the application into clear content areas.
To create visual hierarchy within the application layout.
With the BorderLayout component to define the main content regions of your application with responsive behavior.

props.name: variant
props.description: Styling variant; defaults to "primary".

examples.title: Primary (default)
examples.description: When using panels together, use the primary variant as the background for the most important content that you want the user to focus on first.

examples.title: Secondary
examples.description: Use the secondary variant as the background to color block less important content.

examples.title: Tertiary
examples.description: Use the tertiary variant as an alternative background to the secondary variant or as an additional option to color block in a layout.

preferred_for: To organize and divide the application into clear content areas.
To create visual hierarchy within the application layout.
With the BorderLayout component to define the main content regions of your application with responsive behavior.

components.name: Parent-child layout
components.summary: Parent-childLayout provides a parent content area alongside a dependent child content area and responsively stacks them at a given breakpoint. It is used for interactions where selection in the parent (master) determines detail displayed in the child, such as navigation for a preferences dialog.
when_to_use: To organize content into two areas: a larger area, and a smaller area that controls the larger area.
when_not_to_use: To position more than two layout items along a single dimension. Instead, use FlexLayout, FlowLayout, StackLayout or SplitLayout depending on how you want to position them.
To position more than two layout items in a grid. Instead, use GridLayout.

props.name: child
props.description: Child component to be rendered

props.name: collapseAtBreakpoint
props.description: Breakpoint at which the parent and child will stack.

props.name: gap
props.description: Controls the space between parent and child components, default is 0.

props.name: onCollapseChange
props.description: Function called when the viewport size equal to or less than the collapseAtBreakpoint variable

props.name: parent
props.description: Parent component to be rendered

props.name: visibleView
props.description: View that is displayed when in a collapsed state.

summary: When building a subsection of a page using ParentChildLayout, apply semantic HTML. This means using the correct HTML elements for their intended purpose as much as possible.

rules.rule: When building a subsection of a page using ParentChildLayout, apply semantic HTML. This means using the correct HTML elements for their intended purpose as much as possible.

examples.title: Default
examples.description: A parent-child layout displays the parent component to the left and child component to the right.

examples.title: Collapsed
examples.description: The parent-child layout component changes to a collapsed view once it approaches a specified breakpoint. Only one of the regions remains visible and fills the width of the parent. The breakpoint and visible view can customized using the collapsedAtBreakpoint and visibleView props.

examples.title: Reduced motion
examples.description: You can customize or disable the animations using the prefers-reduced-motion CSS media feature.

examples.title: Preferences dialog
examples.description: This is an example of a preferences dialog using the parent-child layout.

preferred_for: To organize content into two areas: a larger area, and a smaller area that controls the larger area.
not_for: To position more than two layout items along a single dimension. Instead, use FlexLayout, FlowLayout, StackLayout or SplitLayout depending on how you want to position them.
To position more than two layout items in a grid. Instead, use GridLayout.

components.name: Pill
components.summary: Pill is a small visual element that can contain text and an icon. Pills help label, filter or sort interactive content, allowing users to trigger actions or make selections.
when_to_use: To organize selections that don’t necessarily belong to the same category or need to be arranged in a certain way.
To trigger an immediate action such as filtering a view of content by a specific property.
To enable an action that could change depending on the context. In a messaging application, you could use pills to display predefined replies that change depending on the received message.
when_not_to_use: To organize selections belonging to the same category in a clear hierarchical way. Instead, use Checkbox.
To switch between views (e.g., Grid view vs. Card view). Instead, use ToggleButton.
For binary actions that take effect immediately. Instead, use Switch.
For actions that do not dynamically change with the context. Instead, use Button.
For labeling. Instead, use Tag.

props.name: value
props.description: No description provided.

props.name: defaultSelected
props.description: The default selected values for un-controlled component.

props.name: disabled
props.description: If true, the Pill group will be disabled.

props.name: onSelectionChange
props.description: Callback fired when the selection changes. @param event @param newSelected The new selected values.

props.name: selected
props.description: The currently selected values.

props.name: selectionVariant
props.description: Selection variant of the Pill group. If "none", the Pills will not be selectable. If "multiple", pills inside behave like checkboxes.

examples.title: Default
examples.description: Pills are often used for quick actions such as choosing an auto-generated response to a message or reserving a seat in a room.

examples.title: Closable pill
examples.description: A pill can be indicated as closable by adding a close icon on the right side of the pill. This type of pill is often used to represent a value that the user (or system) has selected, and which the user is able to remove from the selection. It can appear on its own, such as alongside a complex filter panel, or within an input control such as ComboBox.

examples.title: Grouped
examples.description: Pills can be grouped together using the PillGroup component.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: We recommend wrapping Selectable pill groups in a FormField component to provide a visible label and optionally help text. - When selectable pill groups don't have a visible label, ensure the group has an accessible name by using aria-label or aria-labelledby.

examples.title: Wrapping
examples.description: A group of selectable pills can wrap for smaller screens or to save space when there are many items.

examples.title: With icons
examples.description: Visually reinforce the theme or action of the pill by using icons.
examples.code:
import { Pill, PillGroup, StackLayout } from "@salt-ds/core";
import {
  CloseIcon,
  GuideClosedIcon,
  UserAdminIcon,
  UserBadgeIcon,
} from "@salt-ds/icons";
import type { ReactElement } from "react";

export const Icon = (): ReactElement => (
  <StackLayout align="center">
    <Pill>
      <UserBadgeIcon aria-hidden />
      Call client
    </Pill>
    <Pill aria-label="Remove jane.smith@jpmchase.com">
      <UserBadgeIcon aria-hidden /> jane.smith@jpmchase.com
      <CloseIcon aria-hidden />
    </Pill>
    <PillGroup aria-label="Select user roles">
      <Pill value="admin">
        <UserAdminIcon aria-hidden /> Admin
      </Pill>
      <Pill value="readOnly">
        <GuideClosedIcon aria-hidden />
        Read-only
      </Pill>
    </PillGroup>
  </StackLayout>
);

      <CallIcon aria-hidden />
    </Pill>
    <Pill aria-label="Remove jane.smith@jpmchase.com" disabled>
      <UserBadgeIcon aria-hidden /> jane.smith@jpmchase.com
      <CloseIcon aria-hidden />
    </Pill>
    <PillGroup

      </Pill>
      <Pill value="readOnly">
        <GuideClosedIcon aria-hidden />
        Read-only
      </Pill>
    </PillGroup>
  </StackLayout>
);

components.name: Progress
components.summary: Progress indicator gives the user a precise view of how long a system operation is taking. Use it when the action will last more than a second. Two variants are available to accommodate different layouts: CircularProgress and LinearProgress.
