components.name: Radio button
components.summary: RadioButton and RadioButtonGroup show a set of mutually exclusive options. Users can only select one radio button at a time. When selecting a new option, the previously selected option automatically clears. Radio buttons should be used to display a choice between two or more options, since users cannot clear a single radio button after selecting it.
when_to_use: When you have fewer than five options and only single selection is necessary.
when_not_to_use: When you have a Boolean selection (on/off). Instead, use Switch.
When you have fewer than five options and multiple selection is necessary. Instead, use CheckboxGroup.
When you have more than five and fewer than 10 options. Instead, use Dropdown.
When you have more than 10 options. Instead, use ComboBox.

examples.description: Keep text short and as concise as possible in the horizontal orientation. - Do not truncate text descriptions as this can hide important content relevant to the user’s workflow. Use a read-only radio button instead of a disabled radio button when the text description contains information valuable to the user.

Any status provided to the group will take precedence over the statuses applied directly to the nested controls. - Avoid using validationStatus with a disabled radio button. If provided, disabled functionality and styling will take precedence over any validation status styling.

components.name: Range date picker
components.summary: RangeDatePicker is composed of both a DateInput and Calendar controls. They enable users to select a date range that consists of a start and end date, offering a flexible and intuitive way to make a selection.
when_to_use: Use a DatePicker component when you need a visual and intuitive interface for selecting single dates or date ranges, making it ideal for financial applications, event scheduling, and project management. In contrast, use a Dropdown when the dates are pre-determined, the selection is infrequent, or a compact interface is required, such as selecting a birth year or a specific month in a form.

summary: Wrap DatePicker in FormField to provide the field with a visible label, help text, and a status message for validation feedback. This will ensure it complies with accessibility guidelines. You can find more information in the W3C form instructions.
Clearly indicate dates that can’t be selected, such as weekends, non-trading days, or other blocked days, if relevant to your use case.
Consider showing date options in a dropdown instead of a date picker if the date or range is pre-determined and cannot be specified by the user.
As the DatePicker typically composes DateInput controls, refer to the accessibility guidance for DateInput.

rules.rule: Wrap DatePicker in FormField to provide the field with a visible label, help text, and a status message for validation feedback. This will ensure it complies with accessibility guidelines. You can find more information in the W3C form instructions.
Clearly indicate dates that can’t be selected, such as weekends, non-trading days, or other blocked days, if relevant to your use case.
Consider showing date options in a dropdown instead of a date picker if the date or range is pre-determined and cannot be specified by the user.
As the DatePicker typically composes DateInput controls, refer to the accessibility guidance for DateInput.

examples.title: Default
examples.description: An uncontrolled DatePicker, composed of a DateInputRange and a Calendar component, allows users to select a date range when the selectionVariant prop is set to "range", without requiring explicit state management in the parent component, making it ideal for selecting start and end dates for various applications.

examples.title: Controlled
examples.description: A controlled DatePicker, composed of a DateInputRange and a Calendar component, allows users to select a date range when the selectionVariant prop is set to "range", with the parent component explicitly managing the start and end date states, providing greater control over the date range selection process.

examples.title: Calendar grid view
examples.description: Use DatePickerRangeGridPanel to provide a grid of multiple calendars, enabling the user to select the date from a wider range of visible months.

examples.title: Calendar grid view
examples.description: The columns and numberOfVisibleMonths properties are responsive, allowing you to adjust the number of calendars or columns at each breakpoint for optimal display on different screen sizes.

examples.title: Within form field
examples.description: A DatePicker with selectionVariant set to "range", inside a FormField, provides the field with a visible label, help text, and a status message for validation feedback.

examples.title: Initial error
examples.description: When used with FormField, the validationStatus prop can be used to recreate an initial error state.

examples.title: Unselectable dates
examples.description: Unselectable dates are visually distinct from selectable dates. When a user attempts to select an unselectable date, the calendar displays a tooltip with additional information. To implement this, use the isDayUnselectable prop to pass a function that determines whether a day is unselectable and provides the tooltip description.

examples.title: Highlighted dates
examples.description: Highlighted dates are marked by a triangle in the top right corner, indicating an event associated with that day. When a user hovers over a highlighted date, the calendar displays a tooltip with additional information. To implement this, use the isDayHighlighted prop to pass a function that determines whether a day is highlighted and provides the tooltip description.

examples.title: Min/max date
examples.description: To constrain the selected date within a specific range, use the minDate and/or maxDate props. If minDate is not provided, it defaults to the year 1900. If maxDate is undefined, it defaults to the year 2100. This ensures that the selected date remains within the specified range.

examples.title: Custom content
examples.description: The composed nature of the DatePicker enables you to control the content of the overlay using the children prop. Use the recommended panels or compose them with your own.

examples.title: With confirmation
examples.description: You can change dates upon selection, or apply the selection with additional controls.

examples.title: Locale
examples.description: When the locale prop is provided, the DatePicker component uses it to define the locale, ensuring that date formats, month names, and other locale-specific elements are displayed according to the specified locale.

examples.title: Timezone
examples.description: Use the timezone prop to display the selected date range in a specific timezone. This is particularly useful for applications that need to handle date and time data across different time zones, ensuring that users can accurately select and view date ranges based on their local time. If you need to work with a date string (e.g., in the YYYY-MM-DD format) and display it using a date picker, you don't need to configure a timezone. Simply use the dateAdapter.format and dateAdapter.parse functions wi

examples.title: Bordered
examples.description: A DatePicker component with a border provides a visually distinct area for selecting dates, enhancing the user interface by clearly delineating the component boundaries. This styling can improve usability and accessibility by making the calendar controls more noticeable and easier to interact with.

preferred_for: Use a DatePicker component when you need a visual and intuitive interface for selecting single dates or date ranges, making it ideal for financial applications, event scheduling, and project management. In contrast, use a Dropdown when the dates are pre-determined, the selection is infrequent, or a compact interface is required, such as selecting a birth year or a specific month in a form.

components.name: Range slider
components.summary: RangeSlider enables the selection of an interval within a range of values. It offers inline label positioning and can display marks under the track.
when_to_use: When selecting a range of numeric values or relative scale.
When the exact value does not matter (depending on the size of the predefined range), but an approximate interval is good enough.
when_not_to_use: When it’s important to select an exact numeric value that falls within a significantly large range, for example, 1-1000. Instead, use Input.
When displaying progress through a series of separate steps, such as a wizard or multi-step form. Use SteppedTracker instead.

props.name: accessibleMaxText
props.description: Accessible text to announce maximum value label.

props.name: accessibleMinText
props.description: Accessible text to announce minimum value label.

props.name: constrainLabelPosition
props.description: When minimum and maximum labels are defined, ensure they are confined within the boundary of the slider.

props.name: decimalPlaces
props.description: The number of allowed decimal places

props.name: defaultValue
props.description: The default value. Use when the component is not controlled.

props.name: disabled
props.description: Disable the slider.

props.name: format
props.description: A callback to format the display value in the tooltip, min and max labels and the aria-valuetext attribute.

props.name: marks
props.description: Marks that are displayed under the track.

props.name: max
props.description: Maximum slider value.

props.name: maxLabel
props.description: Label for maximum value.

props.name: min
props.description: Minimum slider value.

props.name: minLabel
props.description: Label for the minimum value.

props.name: onChange
props.description: Callback called when slider value is changed. It provides a generic event and the current value of the slider.

props.name: onChangeEnd
props.description: Callback called when the slider is stopped from being dragged or its value is changed from the keyboard. It provides a generic event and the current value of the slider.

props.name: restrictToMarks
props.description: Restrict slider value to marks only. The step will be ignored.

props.name: showTicks
props.description: Show visual ticks above the marks.

props.name: showTooltip
props.description: Show the slider value in a tooltip when the thumb is hovered.

props.name: step
props.description: Minimum interval the slider thumb can move.

props.name: stepMultiplier
props.description: Maximum interval the slider thumb can move when using PageUp and PageDown keys.

props.name: value
props.description: Value of the slider, to be used when in a controlled state.

examples.title: Default
examples.description: Selection of an interval of values from a RangeSlider track. A tooltip displays the value of the RangeSlider's "thumbs" when a thumb is focused or hovered.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: To make the RangeSlider accessible, ensure screen readers can accurately convey the range and its values. While screen readers announce the default minimum and maximum values, customizing labels with minLabel and maxLabel is best paired with updating screen reader text using accessibleMinText and accessibleMaxText. These props let you define the exact text announced for the minimum and maximum values, ensuring alignment with custom labels. For instance, if minLabel is "Low" and maxLabel is "High

examples.title: With constrained label position
examples.description: You can use the constrainLabelPosition prop to ensure that the bottom labels remain within the RangeSlider's boundaries. When set to true, it prevents labels from extending beyond the RangeSlider, maintaining a tidy and consistent appearance. This is particularly useful for preserving layout integrity and ensuring label readability across various screen sizes. **Note:** Enabling constrainLabelPosition may lead to inconsistent spacing between marks. Use this option carefully to balance visual cla

examples.title: With marks
examples.description: You can display marks under the RangeSlider track to label specific values. The showTicks property allows you to display small ticks above the marks, enhancing visual guidance for users by providing clear reference points along the track. This feature improves usability by making it easier for users to select precise values. You can combine marks with minLabel and maxLabel to provide even more context regarding the RangeSlider. To add non-numeric marks to certain values, please refer to the exam

examples.title: Restrict to marks
examples.description: The restrictToMarks property, when set to true, confines the Slider's movement to predefined values specified in the marks array. This means that the Slider can only stop at these specific values, rather than any arbitrary value along the Slider's range. The step property is ignored in this case.

examples.title: Disabled
examples.description: You can set the RangeSlider as disabled.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: We recommend (max - min) to be evenly divisible by the step to ensure that all values of the RangeSlider are reachable.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: When formatting the values, it is recommended to add the same formatting to the marks if passed.

examples.title: Form field compatibility
examples.description: You can wrap the RangeSlider in a form field and display a label. For more information, refer to the form field documentation.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: When using inputs, ensure the RangeSlider is controlled (by explicitly setting the value prop). - Use the RangeSlider's onChange callback to update the input values when the RangeSlider is interacted with. - Validate your input value changes and update the validationStatus prop on any inputs accordingly. - Only use the inline labels of the RangeSlider alongside an input. This ensures the size-base alignment of the overall component layout is maintained.

examples.title: Hide tooltip
examples.description: To hide the tooltip when hovering over the RangeSlider thumb, you can use the showTooltip prop. This is beneficial in cases where your RangeSlider already conveys enough information, eliminating the need for a tooltip.

examples.title: Best practices:
examples.description: Create a controlled slider and use your format() callback to create formatted marks. - Update aria-valuetext with the formatted current slider value. - Make the marks responsive on smaller screens by implementing logic to render them based on breakpoints. This approach ensures a user-friendly and accessible interface for selecting non-numeric values.

examples.title: With change events
examples.description: The RangeSlider component provides two key event handlers: onChange and onChangeEnd, which are essential for handling user interactions with the RangeSlider, whether through mouse or keyboard input. - onChange **Event**: This event is triggered continuously as the user drags the RangeSlider thumbs or adjusts them using keyboard controls. It provides real-time feedback by updating the value as the thumbs move or as keyboard inputs modify their value. This is particularly useful for scenarios wher

preferred_for: When selecting a range of numeric values or relative scale.
When the exact value does not matter (depending on the size of the predefined range), but an approximate interval is good enough.
not_for: When it’s important to select an exact numeric value that falls within a significantly large range, for example, 1-1000. Instead, use Input.
When displaying progress through a series of separate steps, such as a wizard or multi-step form. Use SteppedTracker instead.

components.name: Rating
components.summary: Rating component allows users to submit feedback and view average scores on a defined scale, reflecting your product, service or experience.
when_to_use: When you have to provide feedback or indicate your level of satisfaction with a product, service, or experience.
When there are at least 3 increments—this allows users to express a basic range of sentiment (poor, average, good).
when_not_to_use: When there are fewer than 3 increments (such as a binary thumbs up/down or like/dislike) the component doesn’t offer enough nuance for most rating scenarios. In this scenario, use a ToggleButtonGroup.
When there are more than 10 increments and users need to select an option, use a ComboBox.

props.name: defaultValue
props.description: Default rating value for uncontrolled mode.

props.name: disabled
props.description: If true, the rating component will be in a disabled state.

props.name: getLabel
props.description: Function used to provide a user-friendly name for the current value of the rating. Primarily used by screen readers.

props.name: getVisibleLabel
props.description: Function used to provide a visible label for the rating.

props.name: labelPlacement
props.description: Position of the label relative to the rating component. Can be "top", "right", "bottom", or "left".

props.name: max
props.description: Total number of icons displayed.

props.name: name
props.description: The name to be set on each radio button within the group. If not set, then one will be generated for you.

props.name: onChange
props.description: Callback function for rating change. The first parameter is the event, and the second is the selected rating value.

props.name: readOnly
props.description: If true, the rating component will be in a read-only state.

props.name: value
props.description: When provided, the component is controlled.

examples.title: Default
examples.description: By default, the Rating displays five stars, from which users can select a rating.

examples.title: Read-only
examples.description: Use the read-only state when you need to communicate a single user’s or an aggregate rating, and prevent it from being edited. A rating in a read-only state will be submitted with the corresponding form. Note: If you must use a read-only rating, you should always add an accessible name that makes it obvious the switch is read-only, be that via a visual label or an aria-label if there's no space for a visual label.

examples.title: Disabled
examples.description: Use the disabled state for the Rating component when context or user permissions require it. Showing the component in a disabled state indicates its presence and functionality, even though it’s not available.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: If using a label positioned to the left, set a fixed width to avoid the component jumping when moving between selections.

examples.title: Form Field support
examples.description: Rating component is compatible with FormField. However, the component doesn’t include Form Field out-the-box.

examples.title: Best practice
examples.description: It’s recommended to not exceed 10 rating icons for highly detailed feedback as it can be overwhelming, and is unnecessary for most scenarios.

examples.title: Custom icons
examples.description: The default star icon can be replaced with other Salt DS icons depending on your use case. Achieve custom icons in code using the semantic icon provider.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: A live region or an announcement should be used to provide feedback to screen reader users. - The clear button should remain visible and enabled as long as the rating is editable, allowing users to change their input. - If the Rating component is set to read-only, the clear button should be hidden, since users cannot modify or clear the rating in this state. This approach ensures clarity and prevents confusion, as users only see actionable controls when interaction is possible.

preferred_for: When you have to provide feedback or indicate your level of satisfaction with a product, service, or experience.
When there are at least 3 increments—this allows users to express a basic range of sentiment (poor, average, good).
not_for: When there are fewer than 3 increments (such as a binary thumbs up/down or like/dislike) the component doesn’t offer enough nuance for most rating scenarios. In this scenario, use a ToggleButtonGroup.
When there are more than 10 increments and users need to select an option, use a ComboBox.

components.name: Salt provider
components.summary: SaltProvider is a required component for changing the theme, mode, density and breakpoints of your application.

props.name: applyClassesTo
props.description: Either "root", "scope" or "child". Specifies the location of salt theme class and attributes should be applied to. Defaults to "root" for a root provider, otherwise "scope".

props.name: breakpoints
props.description: Shape of { xs: number; sm: number; md: number; lg: number; xl: number; }. Determines breakpoints used in responsive calculation for layout components.

props.name: density
props.description: Either "high", "medium", "low", "touch" or "mobile". Determines the amount of content that can fit on a screen based on the size and spacing of components. Refer to density doc for more detail.

props.name: enableStyleInjection
props.description: A boolean. Enables dynamic style injection for each component. If false, you'll need to include component CSS yourself.

props.name: mode
props.description: Either "light" or "dark". Enable the color palette to change from light to dark. Refer to modes doc for more detail.

props.name: theme
props.description: A string. Specifies custom theme name(s) you want to apply, similar to className.

examples.title: Theme
examples.description: Use SaltProvider to change the theme of your application with the theme prop. You can pass in multiple theme names if needed, just like className. Use the useTheme hook to access the currently active theme.

examples.title: Modes
examples.description: Use the mode prop to change the mode for all nested components. Modes enable the color palette to change from light to dark. You can read more about it on the modes foundation page. The default mode is light mode. You can use the mode toggle at the top of this page to change the mode in the example. Note how the card background and text change. Use the useTheme hook to access the current mode.

examples.title: Density
examples.description: Use the density prop to change the density for all nested components. Density controls the amount of content that can fit on a screen based on the size and spacing of components. You can read more about it on the density foundation page. The default density is medium. You can use the density toggle at the top of this page to change the density and see how the spacing changes between the squares. Use the useDensity hook to access the current density.

examples.title: Responsive density
examples.description: It is recommended to use "touch" density on touchscreen/mobile devices. You can use media queries or other methods to detect devices to achieve this.

examples.title: Breakpoints
examples.description: Use the breakpoints prop to customize the CSS media queries that hooks such as useBreakpoints and useCurrentBreakpoint use internally. You can use these hooks for the responsive props to pass multiple values (one per breakpoint) or a single value that would take affect across all screen sizes. You can read the responsiveness foundation page for more information about Salt's default breakpoints. Use the useBreakpoints hook to access the current breakpoints defined via the Salt Provider. This is a

examples.title: Nested providers
examples.description: You can use SaltProvider to create scoped themes, allowing you to mix themes, modes, densities and breakpoints in your application. Familiarize yourself with the guidance on our foundations pages, where we recommend that you use this sparingly. Nested providers will inherit values from parent providers.

components.name: Scrim
components.summary: Scrim is a temporary, translucent layer that makes underlying content less prominent.
when_to_use: To focus the user's attention on the content of an overlaying component within a specific viewport or content area.
when_not_to_use: When the user needs to interact with the content beneath at the same time.

props.name: fixed
props.description: If true the scrim is bound to the document viewport. The default value of this prop is false, and the default behavior is for Scrim to be bound to its parent container (nearest positioned ancestor).

props.name: open
props.description: If true the scrim is shown.

examples.title: Fill viewport
examples.description: Use the fixed prop to make the scrim fill the viewport.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: Ensure you have explicitly set a position attribute for the parent container.

examples.title: With child
examples.description: You can pass children to a scrim, such as a Spinner to indicate loading state. You can incorporate other elements as children, to display them above the scrim and draw attention to them.

preferred_for: To focus the user's attention on the content of an overlaying component within a specific viewport or content area.
not_for: When the user needs to interact with the content beneath at the same time.

components.name: Segmented button group
components.summary: SegmentedButtonGroup displays a set of actionable buttons that are flush with separators.
when_to_use: Use the segmented button group to display a list of actionable buttons, flush with separators between them.

summary: When using segmented button group ensure you follow the button accessibility guidance.

rules.rule: When using segmented button group ensure you follow the button accessibility guidance.

examples.title: Variants
examples.description: Group buttons of the same variant to indicate that they are related. Buttons within a single segmented button group should all be of the same variant. See the button docs on which variant to use.
examples.code:
import { Button, SegmentedButtonGroup, StackLayout } from "@salt-ds/core";
import { CallIcon, ChatGroupIcon, MessageIcon } from "@salt-ds/icons";
import type { ReactElement } from "react";

export const Variants = (): ReactElement => (
  <StackLayout>
    <SegmentedButtonGroup>
      <Button>
        <MessageIcon /> Message
      </Button>
      <Button>
        <ChatGroupIcon />
        Chat
      </Button>
      <Button>
        <CallIcon /> Call
      </Button>
    </SegmentedButtonGroup>
    <SegmentedButtonGroup>
      <Button appearance="transparent">
        <MessageIcon /> Message
      </Button>
      <Button appearance="transparent">
        <ChatGroupIcon /> Chat
      </Button>
      <Button appearance="transparent">
        <CallIcon /> Call
      </Button>
    </SegmentedButtonGroup>
    <SegmentedButtonGroup>
      <Button sentiment="accented">
        <MessageIcon /> Message
      </Button>
      <Button sentiment="accented">
        <ChatGroupIcon /> Chat
      </Button>
      <Button sentiment="accented">
        <CallIcon /> Call
      </Button>
    </SegmentedButtonGroup>
  </StackLayout>
);

examples.title: Icon Only
examples.description: Avoid using icons that are very similar in the same segmented button group. Use Tooltip on an icon-only button to describe the action, as per the button guidance.
examples.code:
import { Button, SegmentedButtonGroup, Tooltip } from "@salt-ds/core";
import { CallIcon, ChatGroupIcon, MessageIcon } from "@salt-ds/icons";
import type { ReactElement } from "react";

export const IconOnly = (): ReactElement => (
  <SegmentedButtonGroup>
    <Tooltip placement="top" content="Message">
      <Button aria-label="Message">
        <MessageIcon aria-hidden />
      </Button>
    </Tooltip>
    <Tooltip content="Chat" placement="top">
      <Button aria-label="Chat">
        <ChatGroupIcon aria-hidden />
      </Button>
    </Tooltip>
    <Tooltip content="Call" placement="top">
      <Button aria-label="Call">
        <CallIcon aria-hidden />
      </Button>
    </Tooltip>
  </SegmentedButtonGroup>
);

preferred_for: Use the segmented button group to display a list of actionable buttons, flush with separators between them.

components.name: Semantic icon provider
components.summary: SemanticIconProvider centralizes icon management and customization with Salt. It allows flexible configuration of icons and ensures consistent usage across your application.

props.name: children
props.description: Child elements that will use the provided icons.

props.name: iconMap
props.description: Custom mapping of icon names to components. Overrides default icons if provided.

examples.title: Default
examples.description: Use the SemanticIconProvider to replace any existing icon management setup with this provider and provide any custom icon mappings as required.

examples.title: Icon map
examples.description: The SemanticIconProvider allows you to specify an iconMap to manage which icon components are used for various icon names. This provides flexibility to customize the icon set used in your application.

components.name: Skip link
components.summary: SkipLink allows screen reader and keyboard users to bypass menus and jump directly into the main content. This removes accessibility barriers by reducing keystroke repetition and streamlining navigation.
when_to_use: Bypass repetitive navigation or elements at the top of a page, providing direct access to the main content.
Ensure screen reader and keyboard users can efficiently reach key sections of your web application.
Prevent users from repeatedly hearing header and navigation content on every page before accessing the main content.

props.name: targetId
props.description: The ID of the target element to apply focus when the link is clicked. If the element with this ID is not found, the SkipLink will not be rendered.

examples.title: Default
examples.description: Skip links stay hidden ensuring they aren’t intrusive until accessed through keyboard navigation. Once activated, skip links shift focus to the main content of the web application and hide again.

components.name: Spinner
components.summary: Spinner is a visual representation of a process taking indeterminate time to complete. Triggers include automated system processes and user actions. It can also indicate content loading. Displaying a spinner reassures the user that the system is still working, especially when the operation takes longer than one second.
when_to_use: To indicate that content is loading and reassure the user that the system is still working.
For actions that you expect will take 1–9 seconds to load or for processes that will take an indeterminate amount of time to complete.
when_not_to_use: For actions that will run for a determinable length of time. Instead, use Progress.

For a process that is taking longer than 10 seconds to complete, as this is the point at which any attempt for a system to respond should finish, and a status message should display instead. For further guidance, view the Salt duration foundation.

props.name: announcerInterval
props.description: Determines the interval on which the component will continue to announce the aria-label. Defaults to 5000ms (5s)

props.name: announcerTimeout
props.description: Determines the interval after which the component will stop announcing the aria-label. Defaults to 20000ms (20s)

props.name: className
props.description: The className(s) of the component

props.name: completionAnnouncement
props.description: Determines the message to be announced by the component when it unmounts. Set to null if not needed.

props.name: disableAnnouncer
props.description: If true, built in aria announcer will be inactive

props.name: id
props.description: The ids of the SvgSpinner components

props.name: role
props.description: The prop for the role attribute of the component

props.name: size
props.description: Determines the size of the spinner. Must be one of: 'default', 'large', 'small', 'medium'.

summary: Improve accessibility by customizing the aria-label to provide additional context about what is loading, for example, aria-label="loading settings panel".
Screen-reader users will hear the aria-label at a regular interval, which you can customize using the announcerInterval property.
After a set timeout, the component will stop announcing the aria-label, which you can customize using the announcerTimeout property.

Screen-reader users will hear a completion message when the spinner unmounts to indicate that loading has finished. You can customize this using the completionAnnouncement prop or disable it by setting completionAnnouncement={null}.

rules.rule: Improve accessibility by customizing the aria-label to provide additional context about what is loading, for example, aria-label="loading settings panel".
Screen-reader users will hear the aria-label at a regular interval, which you can customize using the announcerInterval property.
After a set timeout, the component will stop announcing the aria-label, which you can customize using the announcerTimeout property.

Screen-reader users will hear a completion message when the spinner unmounts to indicate that loading has finished. You can customize this using the completionAnnouncement prop or disable it by setting completionAnnouncement={null}.

examples.title: Small
examples.description: The small spinner is used for embedding in individual components (e.g., button or input) that require dynamic loading experiences. For the small size set the size prop to "small".

examples.title: Medium (default)
examples.description: You would typically use the medium spinner for widget loading experiences. For the medium size, set the size prop to ”medium” or leave it as undefined.

examples.title: Large
examples.description: The large spinner is useful for full-screen loading experiences. For the large size, set the size prop to ”large”.

examples.title: Loading
examples.description: Center the spinner in the middle of a page or content that's loading.

examples.title: Partial loading
examples.description: When updating only a portion of a page, position the spinner within that specific area.

preferred_for: To indicate that content is loading and reassure the user that the system is still working.
For actions that you expect will take 1–9 seconds to load or for processes that will take an indeterminate amount of time to complete.
not_for: For actions that will run for a determinable length of time. Instead, use Progress.

For a process that is taking longer than 10 seconds to complete, as this is the point at which any attempt for a system to respond should finish, and a status message should display instead. For further guidance, view the Salt duration foundation.

components.name: Split layout
components.summary: SplitLayout is a simple mechanism which arranges UI elements at opposite ends of a container. Use it with vertical or horizontal orientation, to provide visual hierarchy for controls such as buttons or toolbars.
when_to_use: If you need two defined areas at the opposite horizontal ends of a container, for example, if you need two button bar components in a header.
when_not_to_use: If your layout requires more than two regions. Instead, use FlowLayout.
If you want more complex customization in your layout, consider FlexLayout.

props.name: align
props.description: Defines the default behavior for how flex items are laid out along the cross axis on the current line.

props.name: as
props.description: The HTML element used for the root node.

props.name: direction
props.description: Establishes the main-axis, defining the direction children are placed. Default is "row".

props.name: endItem
props.description: End component to be rendered.

props.name: gap
props.description: Controls the space between left and right items.

props.name: margin
props.description: Defines the margin around the component. It can be specified as a number (which acts as a multiplier) or a string representing the margin value. Default is 0.

props.name: padding
props.description: Defines the padding within the component. It can be specified as a number (which acts as a multiplier) or a string representing the padding value. Default is 0.

props.name: ref
props.description: No description provided.

props.name: startItem
props.description: Start component to be rendered.

summary: When building a sub-section of a page using a SplitLayout, apply semantic HTML. This means using the correct HTML elements for their intended purpose as much as possible.
SplitLayout supports semantic HTML with the as prop. This renders the appropriate HTML instead of a plain div.

rules.rule: When building a sub-section of a page using a SplitLayout, apply semantic HTML. This means using the correct HTML elements for their intended purpose as much as possible.
SplitLayout supports semantic HTML with the as prop. This renders the appropriate HTML instead of a plain div.

examples.title: Default
examples.description: Split layouts display items horizontally at either end of the container by default with its items aligned centrally in the layout. The items drive the height and width of the overall layout.

examples.title: Layout direction
examples.description: The direction prop defines the main axis along which items are displayed. By default, direction is set to row, arranging items horizontally. Setting direction to column arranges items vertically along a single line. The values row-reverse and column-reverse can be used to reverse the order of items along the main axis, displaying them in the opposite direction.

examples.title: Button bar
examples.description: This example shows two groups of buttons inside a split layout, such as within an app header. Here, the split layout is in its default configuration.

examples.title: Responsive props
examples.description: A responsive prop is a prop that takes either multiple values (one value per breakpoint), or a single value that affects all screen sizes. For example, a layout that will responsively change direction from row to column at specific breakpoints or viewport sizes: direction={{ xs: "column", sm: "column", lg: "row", md: "row ", xl: "row" }}

examples.title: Layout alignment
examples.description: Control the position of the split layout and its items using the align prop. If there is additional space available around the layout (i.e., above or below the row, or beside the column) use the align prop to position items within that space.

examples.title: Flex item position
examples.description: Use the flex item component's align prop to set an alignment for a specific item in the layout. Use the align prop to change the item’s position along the cross-axis of the layout (i.e., perpendicular to the direction–row or column).

examples.title: Flex item size
examples.description: The flex item component's grow prop enables specific items in the layout to grow and fill any remaining space that is available in the layout: - By default, grow is set to 0 and items will display in their normal size. - If you set an item to 1, it will fill the available space. - If you set an item to 2, it will fill twice as much space as items that have grow set to 1. In contrast to the grow prop, the shrink prop shrinks specific items as small as possible within the layout. By default, shrin

examples.title: Spacing
examples.description: Split layout displays items with a gap between items that is a multiple of the Salt base unit controlled by the gap property and is by default set to 3. The spacing properties padding and margin are set to 0 by default and can be used to control the layout's inner and outer spacing (also as multiples of the Salt base unit).

preferred_for: If you need two defined areas at the opposite horizontal ends of a container, for example, if you need two button bar components in a header.
not_for: If your layout requires more than two regions. Instead, use FlowLayout.
If you want more complex customization in your layout, consider FlexLayout.

components.name: Splitter
components.summary: Splitter allows users to customize their workspace layout by interactively adjusting the boundaries between two or more panels, either horizontally or vertically, depending on the orientation and arrangement of the panels.

Top Left Middle Left Bottom Left Top Right Bottom Right

examples.description: Both the resize handles and the resizable panels offer three background variants. **For the Panel Component:** Use the following class names to apply the desired background variant: - resizable-panel-salt-variant-primary - resizable-panel-salt-variant-secondary - resizable-panel-salt-variant-tertiary **For the PanelResizeHandle Component:** Apply these class names to select the appropriate background variant: - resize-handle-salt-variant-primary - resize-handle-salt-variant-secondary - resize-ha

examples.title: Border
examples.description: There are 4 border directions in the the Salt React Resizable Panels theme: top, bottom, left and right. By default, the PanelResizeHandle components will render with no borders, to add borders to the handles, apply any of the following classNames to PanelResizeHandle: For a PanelGroup with direction vertical: - resize-handle-salt-border-top - resize-handle-salt-border-bottom

examples.title: Border
examples.description: For a PanelGroup with direction horizontal: - resize-handle-salt-border-left - resize-handle-salt-border-right

components.name: Stack layout
components.summary: StackLayout is a simple mechanism to arrange UI elements in a line, either horizontally or vertically. It builds on the FlexLayout component and is best for small-scale layouts where displaying components in a single row or column is necessary. It may benefit from supported visual separators, such as a row of charts or a scrolling side panel comprising multiple sections. For additional control of the size and position of content, use FlexItem for each content item inside StackLayout.
when_to_use: When you need a layout that's consistently horizontal or vertical. If there isn't enough space in the main axis, scrolling the content is acceptable.
For small-scale layouts within the application, such as a stack of cards or charts or a vertical side panel with visually distinct sections.
To change the layout’s direction depending on breakpoints or add visual separation using the stack layout separators.
when_not_to_use: Stack layouts will never wrap. If you want to create a layout in a row that responsively wraps over multiple lines, use FlowLayout instead.
If you want the layout to have more complex customization. In this case, consider FlexLayout.

props.name: align

props.name: as

props.name: direction
props.description: Establishes the main-axis, defining the direction children are placed. Default is "column".

props.name: gap
props.description: Controls the space between items, default is 3.

props.name: margin

props.name: padding
props.description: Defines the padding within the component. It can be specified as a number (which acts as a multiplier) or a string representing the padding value. Default is 0.

props.name: ref
props.description: No description provided.

props.name: separators

StackLayout supports this behavior by allowing access to a prop called as that allows you to render the appropriate HTML instead of a plain div.

examples.title: Default
examples.description: By default, StackLayout displays items in a single column aligned to the start of the layout. The items contained within the layout determine its height and width.

examples.title: Layout direction
examples.description: The direction prop defines the main axis along which items are displayed. By default, direction is set to column, arranging items vertically. Setting direction to row arranges items horizontally. The values row-reverse or column-reverse can be used to reverse the order of items along the main axis, displaying them in the opposite direction.

examples.description: The component allows for separators between items. By default, the separators have a centered alignment when separators={true} but you can customize them to sit on the left or right of items. The separator will span across the layout component if the direction is column or span the height if the direction is row. The Stack Layout gap applies to both sides of the separator.

examples.title: Responsive props
examples.description: A responsive prop takes either multiple values (one value per breakpoint) or a single value that would take effect across all different screen sizes. For example, this is a layout that will responsively change direction from row to column at specific breakpoints or viewport sizes: direction={{ xs: "column", sm: "column", lg: "row", md: "row ", xl: "row" }}

examples.title: Layout alignment
examples.description: Control the position of StackLayout and its items using the align prop. If there's additional space available around the layout (i.e., above or below the row or beside the column), use the align prop to define the position of items within that space.

examples.title: FlexItem position
examples.description: Use the FlexItem align prop to set an alignment for a specific item in the layout. Use the align prop to change the item’s position along the cross axis of the layout (i.e., perpendicular to the direction–row or column).

examples.title: FlexItem size
examples.description: The FlexItem grow prop enables specific items in the layout to grow and fill any remaining space in the layout: - By default, grow is set to 0, and items will be in their "normal" size. - If you set an item to 1, it will fill the available space. - If you set an item to 2, it will fill twice as much space as other items where grow is 1. In contrast to the grow prop, the shrink prop makes a specific item as small as possible within the layout. By default, shrink is 1. If you set an item to 2, tha

examples.title: Spacing
examples.description: Stack layout displays items with a gap between items that is a multiple of the Salt base unit controlled by the gap property and is by default set to 3. The spacing properties padding and margin are set to 0 by default and can be used to control the layout's inner and outer spacing also as multiples of the Salt base unit.

preferred_for: When you need a layout that's consistently horizontal or vertical. If there isn't enough space in the main axis, scrolling the content is acceptable.
For small-scale layouts within the application, such as a stack of cards or charts or a vertical side panel with visually distinct sections.
To change the layout’s direction depending on breakpoints or add visual separation using the stack layout separators.
not_for: Stack layouts will never wrap. If you want to create a layout in a row that responsively wraps over multiple lines, use FlowLayout instead.
If you want the layout to have more complex customization. In this case, consider FlexLayout.

components.name: Static list
components.summary: StaticList manages the layout of non-interactive list items, providing a structured and composable way to display content. It's ideal for use in checkout baskets and patterns such as file upload.
when_to_use: To present a set of static list items. It can be used with a FileDropZone to show the list of files added. It can also be used to create a shopping cart list.
when_not_to_use: If you need to create an interactive list, use the ListBox component.

props.name: children
props.description: The list items to be rendered within the StaticList.

props.name: children
props.description: The content of Static List Item

summary: You must check and test any focusable elements inside the static list content accordingly.

rules.rule: You must check and test any focusable elements inside the static list content accordingly.

examples.title: Default
examples.description: The default Static list allows users to show non-interactive list items.

examples.title: Complex label
examples.description: For complex labels, you can add additional elements to provide further context or descriptions for each list item. **Note:** To ensure correct spacing when using buttons or icons, wrap your static content inside StaticListItemContent.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: Icons should be displayed before labels. - Use icons consistently across all static list items within the same group.

examples.title: With buttons
examples.description: You can use Button to add actions to the StaticListItems. **Note:** Buttons should be associated to the list item content using aria-label or aria-labelledby.

examples.title: With dividers
examples.description: You can separate the list of StaticListItems by adding a Divider component between items.

preferred_for: To present a set of static list items. It can be used with a FileDropZone to show the list of files added. It can also be used to create a shopping cart list.
not_for: If you need to create an interactive list, use the ListBox component.

components.name: Status indicator
components.summary: StatusIndicator is a specialized icon to show info, warning, error or success. Use on its own or within another component (e.g., Tooltip) to help convey a message.
when_to_use: Use the info indicator when you need to highlight general information.
Use the error indicator when communicating a critical issue preventing the user from continuing or completing an action.
Use the warning indicator to inform users of an issue or potential issue related to their current task. Use this for issues that don't prevent the user from continuing or completing their task.
Use the success indicator to confirm that a user's action is successful.

props.name: color
props.description: No description provided.

props.name: size
props.description: Multiplier for the base icon size. Should be a positive integer to conform to the rest of the design system.

props.name: status
props.description: Status indicator to be displayed.

summary: Exclude decorative status indicators (status indicators that repeat the information already expressed by the accompanied text) from the screen reader to avoid repetition. If you're adding a status indicator for decorative purposes, pass aria-hidden=”true” to exclude its label from the screen reader.

rules.rule: Exclude decorative status indicators (status indicators that repeat the information already expressed by the accompanied text) from the screen reader to avoid repetition. If you're adding a status indicator for decorative purposes, pass aria-hidden=”true” to exclude its label from the screen reader.

examples.title: Status
examples.description: Use the status prop to change the displayed status. This will affect both the icon and the color of StatusIndicator. The status indicator offers four severity levels that set a distinctive indicator and color.

examples.title: Size
examples.description: You can control the size of the Status Indicator with the size prop. This number will multiply the base size as described in the size foundation.

examples.title: Labeling
examples.description: We recommend that you add a self-explanatory header/label next to the status indicator to help end users to understand the type of status indicated.
examples.code: import { FlowLayout, Label, StackLayout, StatusIndicator } from "@salt-ds/core"; import type { ReactElement } from "react"; export const Labelling = (): ReactElement => ( );

preferred_for: Use the info indicator when you need to highlight general information.
Use the error indicator when communicating a critical issue preventing the user from continuing or completing an action.
Use the warning indicator to inform users of an issue or potential issue related to their current task. Use this for issues that don't prevent the user from continuing or completing their task.
Use the success indicator to confirm that a user's action is successful.

components.name: Stepper
components.summary: Stepper communicates user progress through a linear process. It gives context about where they are, which steps are completed, how many steps are remaining, and whether errors or warnings have occurred.
when_to_use: When there are separate steps within a process, such as a wizard or a multi-step form.
When you want to split a process into distinct, bite-sized sections that are less daunting than lengthy forms.
when_not_to_use: When there are fewer than three steps in a process.
As navigation; users cannot interact with the steps.

props.name: orientation
props.description: The orientation of the stepper: defaults to horizontal.

examples.title: Horizontal (default)
Vertical
Stage + Status
Limitations
examples.description: Nested steps are only supported in vertical orientation
examples.code:
import { StackLayout, Step, Stepper } from "@salt-ds/core";

export const NestedSteps = () => {
  return (
    <StackLayout>
      <Stepper orientation="vertical">
        <Step label="Step 1" stage="completed">
          <Step label="Step 1.1" stage="completed" />
        </Step>
        <Step label="Step 2" stage="inprogress">
          <Step label="Step 2.1" stage="active" />
          <Step label="Step 2.2" stage="pending">
            <Step label="Step 2.2.1" stage="pending" />
            <Step label="Step 2.2.2" stage="pending" />
            <Step label="Step 2.2.3" stage="pending" />
          </Step>
        </Step>
        <Step label="Step 3">
          <Step label="Step 3.1" stage="pending" />
          <Step label="Step 3.2" stage="pending" />
          <Step label="Step 3.3" stage="pending">
            <Step label="Step 3.3.1" stage="pending" />
            <Step label="Step 3.3.2" stage="pending" />
            <Step label="Step 3.3.3" stage="pending" />
          </Step>
        </Step>
      </Stepper>
    </StackLayout>
  );
};

examples.title: Controlled Stepper
examples.description: The Stepper and Step component are primarily presentational components. If you want to control the state of the Stepper you can use a custom hook.

preferred_for: When there are separate steps within a process, such as a wizard or a multi-step form.
When you want to split a process into distinct, bite-sized sections that are less daunting than lengthy forms.
not_for: When there are fewer than three steps in a process.
As navigation; users cannot interact with the steps.

components.name: Switch
components.summary: Switch is a binary control used as a toggle between two different states. When engaged, the thumb travels within the track to indicate change of state. Use it for adjusting settings, preferences or actions within an application or system.
when_to_use: To present an instantaneous, binary choice between two options that doesn’t require the user to submit or confirm the action. The corresponding action takes effect immediately.
To control a critical action that may require confirmation from the user before applying it. This delay is usually intentional, ensuring that users don't accidentally make unwanted changes.
when_not_to_use: To present a list of independent options where the user can select any number of choices. Instead, use Checkbox.
To make a single selection between mutually exclusive choices between two or more options. Instead, use RadioButton.
To toggle between two (or more) opposing yet mutually exclusive states or options with visual priority. Instead, use ToggleButton.
To present multiple options in a group within a compact UI, or if they’re subject to change depending on context. Instead, use selectable Pill.

props.name: checked
props.description: If true, the switch will be checked.

props.name: defaultChecked
props.description: Whether the switch component is checked by default This will be disregarded if checked is already set.

props.name: disabled
props.description: If true, the switch will be disabled.

props.name: inputProps
props.description: Properties applied to the input element.

props.name: label
props.description: The label to be shown next to the switch.

props.name: name
props.description: The name applied to the input.

props.name: onBlur
props.description: Callback when switch loses focus.

props.name: onChange
props.description: Callback when checked state is changed.

props.name: onFocus
props.description: Callback when switch gains focus.

props.name: readOnly
props.description: If true, the switch will be read-only.

props.name: value
props.description: The value of the switch.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: Only use the checked prop for a controlled switch that requires some logic for validating value. - We recommend that you always use a text description for a switch.

examples.title: Checked
examples.description: When defaultChecked={true}, switch is in a checked state, and the switch thumb sits to the right side of the track.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: Use a disabled state for switches that have permissions, dependencies or prerequisites. For example, you can disable a switch in a form because the user hasn't yet completed an earlier section of the form.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: Use a disabled checked state for switches that have permissions, dependencies or pre-requisites. For example, a switch in a form may be in a checked state but disabled as the user doesn't have permissions to edit their selection during a later stage in a form.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: Read-only switches are navigable using keyboard shortcuts. This means that, unlike disabled switches, users can interact with the text description. Use a read-only switch instead of a disabled switch when the text description contains information that is valuable to the user.

examples.title: Left aligned label
examples.description: Use a form field if you need a left aligned label, avoid also having a switch label.

preferred_for: To present an instantaneous, binary choice between two options that doesn’t require the user to submit or confirm the action. The corresponding action takes effect immediately.
To control a critical action that may require confirmation from the user before applying it. This delay is usually intentional, ensuring that users don't accidentally make unwanted changes.
not_for: To present a list of independent options where the user can select any number of choices. Instead, use Checkbox.
To make a single selection between mutually exclusive choices between two or more options. Instead, use RadioButton.
To toggle between two (or more) opposing yet mutually exclusive states or options with visual priority. Instead, use ToggleButton.
To present multiple options in a group within a compact UI, or if they’re subject to change depending on context. Instead, use selectable Pill.

components.name: System status
components.summary: SystemStatus communicates essential platform-wide information, such as maintenance or outage alerts. These messages are displayed to be noticed immediately, prompting users to consider taking action based on four severity levels (with corresponding icons and colors): info, success, warning and error.
when_to_use: To inform users about critical system-wide issues, security updates or vulnerabilities that require immediate attention.
To notify users about new system-wide features or enhancements.
To alert users about planned maintenance schedules and potential service disruptions affecting the entire platform.
when_not_to_use: For general updates or information that requires immediate user action.
For inline messaging. Instead, use Banner.
To show a notification that applies to the user’s current task or workflow. Use Banner instead.
To communicate when content is loading, instead use the Content Status pattern.
When the notification requires immediate action and relates to the user’s current task. Instead, use Dialog to interrupt the user's workflow.

props.name: status
props.description: A string to determine the current state of the SystemStatus. Defaults to info.

props.name: children
props.description: The content of SystemStatusActions

props.name: children
props.description: The content of SystemStatusContent

summary: If the status is critical and requires immediate attention, apply role="alert" to ensure the message is announced immediately.
If the status is not critical, consider applying role="region".

rules.rule: If the status is critical and requires immediate attention, apply role="alert" to ensure the message is announced immediately.
If the status is not critical, consider applying role="region".

examples.title: Info
examples.description: Use the info system status to communicate important updates or new features that do not require immediate action.

examples.title: Error
examples.description: Use error system status for critical issues that affect the user's ability to access or use the platform. When relevant, add a secondary action to help resolve the issue.

examples.title: Warning
examples.description: Warnings announce scheduled outages or events that might impact the product experience, but don't prevent the user from using the application or completing their current workflow. Use warning system status to alert users about potential issues.

examples.title: Success
examples.description: Use success system status to communicate successfully completed system actions.

examples.title: With Title
examples.description: Use a title in a system status to highlight important information, and use description text to provide supporting details.

examples.title: Placement
examples.description: System status alerts are displayed at the very top of the screen, extending across the full width of the page. They are positioned at the same level as the main content, causing any subsequent content to shift accordingly. For example, when a system status is dismissed, the content below it would move up to its original position.

preferred_for: To inform users about critical system-wide issues, security updates or vulnerabilities that require immediate attention.
To notify users about new system-wide features or enhancements.
To alert users about planned maintenance schedules and potential service disruptions affecting the entire platform.
not_for: For general updates or information that requires immediate user action.
For inline messaging. Instead, use Banner.
To show a notification that applies to the user’s current task or workflow. Use Banner instead.
To communicate when content is loading, instead use the Content Status pattern.
When the notification requires immediate action and relates to the user’s current task. Instead, use Dialog to interrupt the user's workflow.

components.name: Table
components.summary: Table is a basic HTML component intended for simple, structured tabular data display. Use it for read-only or lightly formatted tables; if you need sorting, filtering, editing, selection, frozen columns, or custom cell renderers, use Data grid instead.
when_to_use: When you need to present simple data in a structured, tabular format.
When the content is best read row by row, without interactive behaviors such as sorting, filtering, editing, selection, or virtualization.
when_not_to_use: When you need sorting, filtering, editing, selection, pinned columns, or custom cell renderers, use Data grid.
When the table is acting as a data-analysis surface rather than a simple presentation surface, use Data grid.

props.name: divider
props.description: Divider styling variant. Defaults to "tertiary";

props.name: variant
props.description: Styling variant. Defaults to "primary".

props.name: zebra
props.description: Zebra styling. Applies a different fill to every other row.

summary: Use a element (preferred method) or apply aria-labelledby / aria-label attributes to the table
The caption should clearly describe the table's content and purpose

rules.rule: Use a element (preferred method) or apply aria-labelledby / aria-label attributes to the table
The caption should clearly describe the table's content and purpose

examples.title: Basic
examples.description: The table component is used to display tabular data in a structured format. All table elements are supported (, , , etc.).

examples.title: Body variant
examples.description: Table is available in several variants: primary, secondary and tertiary. The variant prop controls the background color of the table. This can be used to create different levels of hierarchy within your UI. The divider prop can be used to adjust the appearance of the dividers in the table. It accepts: primary, secondary, tertiary or none.

examples.title: Zebra
examples.description: The zebra prop adds alternating row colors to improve readability. This is particularly useful for large datasets where distinguishing between rows can be challenging. The color of the alternating rows is determined by the variant prop: - primary variant uses the secondary background for alternating rows. - secondary variant uses the primary background for alternating rows. - tertiary variant uses the primary background for alternating rows. The divider prop can be used to adjust the appearance

examples.title: Header variant
examples.description: The variant prop can also be applied to the table header. This allows you to create a visual distinction between the header and the body of the table. The divider prop can be used to adjust the appearance of the dividers in the table. It accepts: primary, secondary, tertiary or none.

examples.title: Footer variant
examples.description: The variant prop can also be applied to the table footer. This allows you to create a visual distinction between the footer and the body of the table. The divider prop can be used to adjust the appearance of the dividers in the table. It accepts: primary, secondary, tertiary or none.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: Numeric data should be right-aligned to improve readability.

preferred_for: When you need to present simple data in a structured, tabular format.
When the content is best read row by row, without interactive behaviors such as sorting, filtering, editing, selection, or virtualization.
not_for: When you need sorting, filtering, editing, selection, pinned columns, or custom cell renderers, use Data grid.
When the table is acting as a data-analysis surface rather than a simple presentation surface, use Data grid.

components.name: Tabs
components.summary: Tabs allow users to move between different views of related content without leave the current page. A tab strip includes at least two tabs. Only one tab is actively engaged at a time and is differentiated from inactive tabs by a colored indicator either on the top or bottom edge of the tab, depending on the variant. Tab strips can cover the full width of the page or be limited to content areas within components such as dialogs, cards, and drawers.
when_to_use: Use tabs to organize logically related but mutually exclusive content on a single page.
Use tabs to prevent users from navigating to another page to complete a task.
when_not_to_use: Don’t use tabs when users need to compare information between views.
Don’t use tabs to communicate progress. Instead, use Stepper.
Don’t use tabs for primary navigation, taking the user off the current page. Instead, use Navigation Item.

props.name: defaultValue
props.description: The default value. Use when the component is not controlled.

props.name: onChange
props.description: Callback fired when the selection changes. The event will be null when selection is moved automatically.

props.name: value
props.description: The value. Use when the component is controlled.

summary: You need to check and test any focusable elements included in the tab content.

rules.rule: You need to check and test any focusable elements included in the tab content.

examples.title: Appearance
examples.description: Two different appearances are available for tabs: Bordered and Transparent.

examples.title: Divider and inset
examples.description: Tabs can be shown with a divider or inset to help create visual alignment in your UI,

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: Ensure that icon descriptors are used consistently across all tabs within the strip. This helps keep the tabs within the strip equally weighted.

examples.title: With badge
examples.description: Tabs can feature a badge that serves as a visual cue. They can inform users of additional context, notifications, or new content.

examples.title: Overflow
examples.description: When there are too many tabs to fit in the tabstrip, an overflow menu appears. The overflow menu contains a list of all tabs that are not visible in the tabstrip. Users can access the overflow menu by clicking the chevron icon on the right side of the tabstrip or navigating using the arrow keys.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: Provide enough space to display several newly added tabs before overflow is enabled. - Set the new tab to be the active tab when it is added. - useAriaAnnouncer should be used to provide feedback to screen reader users.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: Don't use closable tabs for tabs that are essential to the user's workflow. - Don't allow users to close the last tab in a tabstrip. - useAriaAnnouncer should be used to provide feedback to screen reader users.

examples.title: Disabled tabs
examples.description: Tabs can be set as disabled using the disabled prop.

examples.title: Active color
examples.description: Tabs supports three active colors: "primary", "secondary" and "tertiary".

preferred_for: Use tabs to organize logically related but mutually exclusive content on a single page.
Use tabs to prevent users from navigating to another page to complete a task.
not_for: Don’t use tabs when users need to compare information between views.
Don’t use tabs to communicate progress. Instead, use Stepper.
Don’t use tabs for primary navigation, taking the user off the current page. Instead, use Navigation Item.

components.name: Tag
components.summary: Tag displays metadata categorizing or grouping content in an application. They are read-only and do not interfere with page interactions but use categorical colors to draw attention. Tags appear as stylized elements with text, optionally accompanied by an icon to reinforce their meaning.
when_to_use: For displaying categories in a catalogue or within a group.
To display non-interactive data on a page.
when_not_to_use: For quick filtering and selection, use Pill. Tags and Pills both categorize content in an app, but while Tags are read-only and simply display metadata, Pills are interactive and allow users to filter or sort content.
To display counts, notifications or if you need a small, compact piece of information to be displayed inline, for example ‘New’. Instead, use Badge.

components.name: Text
components.summary: Text component facilitates use of Salt typography styles throughout a product or application, ensuring consistency and unified look and feel. It includes attributes such as font size, weight, letter spacing, line height and decoration.

summary: We're letting you decide on the user experience you'd like to implement for text. However, you need to remain ADA compliant and provide the correct visual and auditory experience for text truncation. Truncated text must comply with WCAG SC 1.4.4.

The requirement states you must make any visually rendered text accessible and display it in full. You must ensure that users can view truncated strings in full if they wish. Consider using tooltips as a mechanism for expanding the text or a link to open the full content on a new page.
If you use truncated text, you must ensure that a screen reader can read the full value.

examples.title: Styling

You can style headings to visually appear as different heading levels, irrespective of the underlying DOM element of a heading on the page.

examples.title: Color
examples.description: Use the color prop to adjust the foreground color of any nested text. Use the primary color most of the time, the secondary color for supporting text or to create visual hierarchy, and inherit to inherit text color from the parent element. Read our guidance on how to use text color.

examples.title: Font weight

examples.title: Variant
examples.description: **Note:** This prop is deprecated. Use the color prop instead. Use the variant prop to adjust the foreground color of any nested text. Use the primary variant most often, and the secondary variant for supporting text or creating a visual hierarchy. Read our guidance on how to use text color.

components.name: Toast
components.summary: Toast displays a small pop-up notification with short feedback in response to a user action. It can also contextualize a system event in a workflow or application outside the user’s current focus. Toasts communicate new information, errors, warnings or successful completion of a process.
when_to_use: To notify users of an event that’s occurred in a peripheral application or workflow. The user should notice the toasts without disrupting their experience or requiring immediate action.
To communicate low-priority information caused by events. Toasts usually don’t require feedback from the user.
To communicate information from a workflow external to the user’s current task or view. Toasts are in an un-intrusive location on an interface.

To show system-generated alerts, independent of the user’s actions. These toasts are persistent and must allow the user to either take action or dismiss them. Toasts can contain customizable content, such as actionable buttons.
when_not_to_use: To show a notification that applies to the user’s current task. Instead, use Banner. Banner sits either on the top of a page or component or inline and usually addresses issues inside the user’s current content area.
When the notification requires immediate action and relates to the user’s current task. Instead, use Dialog to interrupt the user's workflow.

props.name: icon
props.description: (Optional) if provided, this icon component will be used instead of the status icon

props.name: status
props.description: A string to determine the current state of the Toast.

props.name: children
props.description: The content of Toast Content

summary: You must check and test any focusable elements inside the toast content accordingly.

rules.rule: You must check and test any focusable elements inside the toast content accordingly.

examples.title: Default toast
examples.description: Use a toast without a status when you need to display general information in a neutral tone.

examples.title: Info toast
examples.description: Use the info toast when you need to display general information with additional emphasis. An info icon displays alongside a title and supporting message.

examples.title: Error toast
examples.description: Use the error toast to communicate a critical issue. An error icon displays alongside a title and supporting message.

examples.title: Warning toast
examples.description: Use the warning toast to inform users of an issue or potential issue. A warning icon displays alongside a title and supporting message.

examples.title: Success toast
examples.description: Use the success toast to communicate the successful completion of an action. A success icon displays alongside a title and supporting message.

examples.title: Custom Icon
examples.description: Use the icon prop to customize the icon shown in the toast, the custom icon will inherit the status color by default.

To communicate low-priority information caused by events. Toasts usually don’t require feedback from the user.
To communicate information from a workflow external to the user’s current task or view. Toasts are in an un-intrusive location on an interface.

To show system-generated alerts, independent of the user’s actions. These toasts are persistent and must allow the user to either take action or dismiss them. Toasts can contain customizable content, such as actionable buttons.

When the notification requires immediate action and relates to the user’s current task. Instead, use Dialog to interrupt the user's workflow.

components.name: Toggle button
components.summary: ToggleButton allows users to switch states, typically representing on/off or active/inactive status. The corresponding action takes effect immediately. With ToggleButtonGroup, users can make a single choice from a set of related commands, with only one option selected at a time.
when_to_use: To make a single selection when switching between opposing yet mutually exclusive options.
To visually prioritize options higher than RadioButton or Switch.
when_not_to_use: To present an instantaneous or binary choice between two options. Instead, use Switch.
When the options in the group are not high priority and high affordance is not necessary. Instead, use RadioButtonGroup.
When all options in the group don’t need to be visible at once. Consider using Dropdown.

props.name: appearance
props.description: The appearance of the toggle button when selected is true.

props.name: defaultSelected
props.description: Whether the toggle button is selected by default. This will be disregarded if selected is already set.

props.name: onChange
props.description: Callback fired when the toggle button's selection state is changed.

props.name: readOnly
props.description: If true, the toggle button will be read-only.

props.name: selected
props.description: Whether the toggle button is in a selected state.

props.name: sentiment
props.description: The sentiment of the toggle button.

props.name: value
props.description: Value of the toggle button, to be used when in a controlled state.

props.name: appearance
props.description: The appearance of all the toggle buttons within the group.

props.name: defaultValue
props.description: The default value. Use when the component is not controlled.

props.name: disabled
props.description: If true, the Toggle Button Group will be disabled.

props.name: onChange
props.description: Callback fired when the selection changes.

props.name: orientation
props.description: The orientation of the toggle buttons.

props.name: readOnly
props.description: If true, the toggle button group will be read-only.

props.name: sentiment
props.description: The visual sentimenent of all the toggle buttons within the group.

props.name: value
props.description: Value of the toggle button group, to be used when the component is controlled.

summary: When a toggle button has both an icon and text, use aria-hidden on the icon so that screen readers don't announce its text.
When a toggle button has an icon only and no text, pass an aria-label to the button that describes its purpose.

rules.rule: When a toggle button has both an icon and text, use aria-hidden on the icon so that screen readers don't announce its text.
When a toggle button has an icon only and no text, pass an aria-label to the button that describes its purpose.

examples.title: Best practices
examples.description: To reduce visual noise and mitigate alignment issues, use icons in toggle button labels consistently across all options in a toggle button group. - A toggle button group should always contain two or more options related by function or context. For example, text alignment (left, center, right, justified) or risk level (high, medium, low).

examples.title: Group orientation
examples.description: By default, a toggle button group has horizontal alignment. To present the options in a stacked layout, orientate the group vertically using the orientation=”vertical” prop. When oriented vertically, the group will resize to fit the longest toggle button, with all other buttons in the group extending to fill the same width.
